These facts are only sound to emit if the frontend obligation is actually true (F003 negative scope; corpus misleading-comparison #5: 'IR mechanisms are conditional carriers of source facts'). A compiler-verified source contract (Rust `&mut` exclusivity, Fortran dummy-argument rules, SPARK ownership/anti-aliasing — F001, F005, F006) discharges that obligation by construction. A profile-derived 'fact' discharges nothing: to use it soundly you must add guards and invalidation machinery (J002), which is precisely the runtime apparatus a pure LLVM AOT pipeline does not have and which this position rejects. (2) Performance: the well-attested optimizer wins in this corpus — register promotion, redundant-load and dead-store elimination, reordering (F002) — flow from proven non-interference around writes. Contracts deliver them unconditionally, in every compilation, from the first instruction executed. The speculative channel delivers them conditionally and at documented cost: guard checks and de-specialization counters (J002), warmup and code-cache memory spent running a profiling tier before final code exists (J005), and optimizations that are 'when analysis succeeds' rather than guaranteed — HotSpot escape analysis eliminates allocations only when inlining/profile context cooperates and explicitly does not do stack allocation (F007, J004). (3) Transparency: a performance-transparent language promises that reading the source tells you what machine code you get. Speculative systems structurally break this promise: V8 performance depends on shape stability, transition chains, and one-directional element-kind generalization — runtime history, invisible in source, with cliff-shaped degradation under polymorphism and shape churn (J003, J002). You cannot be performance-transparent and history-dependent at once. The architectural corollary is F009: Swift SIL shows that performance-critical semantics (ownership, ARC) should be carried from source into an optimizer-visible IR rather than buried in opaque runtime effects — the static-contract pipeline generalizes this: source contract → semantic IR fact → LLVM attribute/metadata, with soundness preserved at each step. SPARK is the existence proof that very strong contracts (ownership transfer, anti-aliasing, side-effect-free expressions) can be statically enforced (F006), and this language's charter explicitly permits paying the ergonomic cost that stopped SPARK-style discipline from spreading to general-purpose languages. Finally, the steelman of 'predictability is a performance feature': every fact made static deletes an entire cost category — the guard on the fast path, the deopt metadata, the profiling tier, the recompilation — not just on average but in the tail and at startup (J002, J005). citedEvidence.howItSupports: Establishes the framing premise: dynamic runtimes (CPython PEP 659, V8, PyPy) must recover via profiling+guards the same information static languages express in types/contracts. The optimizer's information demand is channel-independent, so the debate reduces to channel quality — where the static channel is guaranteed and the speculative channel is reconstructed. (Provisional direct-extraction card.)

Documents the concrete price of the speculative channel: de-specialization counters, deopt marking when assumptions break, one-directional element-kind generalization. These are exactly the mechanisms a fully static language avoids by making the facts source contracts. (Provisional; its negative scope is conceded below.)

HotSpot tiered compilation trades code-cache memory and a profiling tier for startup — direct evidence that profile-driven optimization has inherent warmup and memory costs. 'No warmup' is not free in the speculative model; it is free in the static model. (Provisional, single-implementation.)

Workflow-confirmed: HotSpot escape analysis yields conditional allocation elimination and lock elision — dependent on inlining, profiles, and implementation limits — not source-level guarantees. This is the anti-transparency property: the same source may or may not get the optimization depending on runtime context.

Primary-vendor sharpening of F007: Oracle explicitly states escape analysis does not replace heap allocation with stack allocation. Runtime analysis systematically under-delivers relative to what a source-level allocation/escape contract could guarantee an AOT compiler. (Provisional direct-extraction card.)

V8 optimizes best under monomorphic shapes and packed element kinds and degrades under shape churn and polymorphism — stable layout is the optimizer-relevant property, and dynamic runtimes reconstruct it fallibly from history while a static language can simply guarantee it in the type system. Also shows speculative performance is a function of runtime history, which source-level transparency cannot express. (Provisional; V8-specific.)

Workflow-confirmed core: language-level non-aliasing/non-interference rules (Rust &mut exclusivity, Fortran dummy-argument rules, SPARK anti-aliasing, __restrict__) are central optimizer enablers — proof that source contracts can carry the highest-value optimizer facts without any runtime machinery.

Specifies the payoff of the statically-carried fact: proven non-interference around writes enables register promotion, redundant-read elimination, dead-store elimination, and reordering. These wins accrue unconditionally when the fact is a contract, versus conditionally when it must be speculated.

The LLVM target has a native vocabulary for static facts (noalias attributes, alias.scope/noalias metadata) whose violation is UB — meaning the emitting frontend must be able to prove the fact. Compiler-checked source contracts are the sound discharge of that proof obligation; profiles are not, absent guard/deopt machinery LLVM AOT lacks.

LLVM noalias return is stronger than C99 restrict — an allocation-like disjointness contract. Shows the static channel is not merely equal to runtime discovery but can express facts stronger than what C-family sources or profiles provide, if the language supplies lifetime/provenance/escape rules — i.e., more source contracts, not fewer.

Fortran demonstrates decades-deployed static aliasing contracts: compilers may optimize assuming dummy-argument rules, and non-TARGET variables are excluded from pointer alias sets. A working precedent that whole-language static no-alias defaults are viable for a performance language.

SPARK is the existence proof for the hard end of my position: statically enforced ownership with transfer, prohibition of non-benign mutable aliasing, and side-effect-free expressions. Since this language's charter explicitly sacrifices ergonomics, SPARK's usability cost — the standard objection — is discounted by design.

Swift SIL shows the architecture my position implies: preserve source-level performance semantics in an optimizer-visible IR (SSA + high-level semantics + explicit ARC) so facts flow source → semantic IR → LLVM, rather than being reconstructed by a runtime. Supports contracts-as-IR-facts as the proven pipeline shape.

concessions: J002's negative scope is explicit and I must own it: deoptimization also enables optimizations static compilers cannot legally perform without profiles. Speculation can exploit facts that are true-in-this-run but not provable in source (actually-monomorphic call sites, run-specific value distributions). A fully static language forfeits these wins unless the source can express them, and the corpus does not show the forfeited amount is small.

J001's negative scope: the corpus does not quantify the residual cost of guards versus static knowledge. My cost argument is mechanism-level (the guards, deopt metadata, and warmup demonstrably exist), not magnitude-level. If guard costs are near-zero on modern hardware for the target workloads, much of my performance case narrows to predictability alone.

The corpus's own misleading-comparisons file forbids me from claiming 'GC/JIT is bad for performance' — managed runtimes conditionally recover real performance (F007, J004). My claim is strictly about conditionality and transparency, not that speculative systems are categorically slower.

The corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection, not by established importance (caveats.md). My position is well-evidenced for non-interference facts; for dispatch, data layout, numerics, and concurrency facts it is extrapolation.

Contracts are only sound if checked. F001's negative scope notes restrict-like contracts depend on programmer truthfulness or unsafe assumptions. Since soundness may not be sacrificed, my position commits the language to compiler-verified contracts (Rust/SPARK-style), which is far more expensive to design and implement than mere annotation — unchecked contracts would just convert speculation risk into UB risk, arguably worse.

'Predictability is itself a performance feature' is partly normative. The corpus documents the mechanisms of unpredictability (J002, J003, J005) but contains no benchmark valuing predictability; I hold that position on charter grounds (performance transparency is a stated design goal), not on measured evidence.

Hint-only PGO (profile-guided block layout, inlining priorities, hot/cold splitting with no guards, no deopt, no runtime machinery) does not introduce unsound facts — it only reorders heuristics. My assigned hardline position excludes even this, and I cannot cite corpus evidence justifying that exclusion; it is the weakest edge of my position.
risks: Expressiveness ceiling: some optimizer-relevant facts are genuinely dynamic (input distributions, data-dependent branch behavior, phase changes). A fully static language must either forgo them or force source-level multiversioning, whose code-size and compile-time costs are unquantified in the corpus.

Contract burden backfire: if verified contracts are too hard to state, users will reach for unsafe/assume escape hatches (the F001 truthfulness problem), reintroducing UB through the back door and undermining the soundness rationale that motivated the position.

Verification cost and feasibility: SPARK-style enforcement is verification-oriented and may not scale to a general-purpose performance language without prohibitive prover effort or rejecting large classes of useful programs (F006 negative scope). The charter tolerates ugliness, but there is a point where unwritable is different from ugly.

Selection-biased corpus: the speculative side is represented only by provisional J-cards, and domains where JITs demonstrably shine (open-world dynamic loading, long-running polymorphic servers) have no verified coverage. My position may look stronger than it is because the corpus was gathered aliasing-first (caveats.md).

No quantitative evidence anywhere: every card is documentation/specification-grounded; none benchmarks anything (caveats.md). A single credible measurement showing profile-derived facts beating contract-derived facts on target workloads would materially weaken this position, and the corpus cannot currently rebut it.

The hardline no-PGO stance may be strictly dominated by 'static contracts for facts + sound profile hints for heuristics,' which preserves soundness and near-total predictability; if the debate frames that hybrid as the opposing position, my marginal territory is only the hint layer, which I cannot defend with cards.
evidenceGaps: Quantified residual cost of guards/deopt metadata versus static knowledge on optimizer-first target workloads (explicitly flagged as unquantified in J001/J002 negative scope). Needed: microbenchmarks and end-to-end measurements of guard overhead in hot loops.

Quantified value of profile-only optimizations a static compiler cannot legally perform (J002 negative scope): how much performance do speculative systems gain from run-specific facts on AOT-style workloads (numeric kernels, systems code) rather than dynamic-language workloads?

Hint-only AOT PGO (BOLT/instrumented-PGO-style layout and inlining, no runtime machinery): zero corpus coverage. This is the strongest challenge to the 'no profile-driven anything' boundary and must be evidenced before the position can win in full strength.

Cost side of static contracts: compile-time, prover effort, rejection rate, and multiversioning code-size for SPARK/Rust-strength contract systems applied to a general performance language (F006 negative scope). Documentation evidence never quantifies this (caveats.md).

Coverage gaps named in caveats.md: no verified evidence on Julia, C#, Mojo, Halide, ISPC, MLIR, numerics, memory models, or concurrency — several of which are exactly the AOT-with-optional-JIT or contract-heavy designs this debate needs comparators for.

Whether static type/layout contracts can fully substitute for value-dependent runtime facts like V8 element-kind specialization (J003) in an AOT setting, or whether AOT languages simply lack a counterpart and eat the loss.

The N001-N006, A001-A010, D001-D010, and C001-C006 evidence cards referenced in the debate brief were not present in the five corpus files I was directed to read; any argument depending on them is unavailable to me and their content could strengthen or weaken this case.

result.position: Selective speculation runtime: guarded specialization (shape/type/value) is permitted ONLY at explicitly designated module boundaries where static contracts are impractical, with sound deoptimization machinery as the escape hatch. Everywhere else, static source contracts remain the default and primary channel of optimizer facts. Speculation regions, their guard sets, and their deopt points are visible in source, preserving the optimizer-first language's performance transparency in kind if not in degree.
result.coreArgument: (1) The optimizer's information demand is channel-independent. J001 documents that what static languages express in types, dynamic runtimes must recover via profiling plus guards — the fact demand is identical, only the delivery channel differs. So the real design question is not 'contracts vs speculation' but 'which channel can deliver which fact, soundly, at which program point.' (2) Some optimizer-relevant facts are structurally unreachable by static contracts: data arriving through FFI or deserialization, dynamically loaded modules/plugins, values (sizes, dispatch targets, branch skew) that exist only at runtime, and call sites whose monomorphic behavior is a property of the deployment, not the source. At those boundaries an all-static language has exactly two options: assume the worst forever (a permanent optimization cliff), or let the programmer assert an unverifiable contract. The corpus shows the second option is a soundness trap: restrict-like contracts 'depend on programmer truthfulness or unsafe assumptions' (F001 negative scope) and violating LLVM's no-alias promises is undefined behavior (F003). A failed guard, by contrast, is a slow path — never UB. Given this debate's ground rule that ergonomics may be sacrificed but soundness may not, guarded speculation at these boundaries is the only option that preserves both soundness and the possibility of optimization; it strictly dominates unverifiable assertions there. (3) Speculation is not merely a compensating mechanism — it adds optimization power static compilers legally cannot have: J002's own scope note records that 'deoptimization also enables optimizations static compilers cannot legally do without profiles; not purely a cost.' (4) The mechanism is proven by convergent evolution: three independent production runtimes (CPython PEP 659, V8, PyPy) all converged on observe/specialize/guard (J001), and V8's machinery identifies precisely which properties speculation should target — stable shapes, monomorphic sites, packed element kinds (J003). A designated-boundary design imports exactly that targeting knowledge without importing whole-program dynamism. (5) Selectivity bounds the documented costs. The corpus's real evidence against runtime machinery — invalidation/deopt bookkeeping (J002), warmup and code-cache memory (J005), conditional-rather-than-guaranteed results (J004, F007) — describes whole-program JIT runtimes. Under my position these are per-region costs, paid only where the alternative is no fact at all, and the static core keeps the corpus's best-verified enablers (F001, F002, F005, F006) untouched. Guard-established dynamic facts can then feed the same optimizations that static non-interference feeds — register promotion, load/store elimination, reordering (F002) — because once a guard has checked the precondition, the specialized region holds the fact just as a contract-bearing region would (the lowering-soundness obligation is real and listed under gaps). This is the steelman: not 'JIT everything,' but 'never force a choice between a permanent optimization cliff and an unsound programmer promise when a checked, deoptimizable assumption is available.'

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Establishes channel-equivalence: CPython/V8/PyPy convergently recover via profiling+guards the same facts static languages express in types — showing guarded specialization is a legitimate, independently-reinvented delivery channel for optimizer facts where static typing cannot reach.

Its negative scope is my strongest single card: 'Deoptimization also enables optimizations static compilers cannot legally do without profiles; not purely a cost.' Also documents that the invalidation machinery is concrete and well-understood (counters, code marking), i.e., an engineerable mechanism, not speculation-in-the-pejorative-sense.

Identifies the optimizer-relevant property (stable shape / monomorphism / packed kinds) that speculation must target and that static languages guarantee elsewhere — this tells a selective design exactly what to guard on at boundaries, rather than guessing.

Its negative scope shows the failure mode of the all-static alternative at hard boundaries: restrict-like contracts 'depend on programmer truthfulness or unsafe assumptions.' Where a contract cannot be verified, a guard is the sound substitute. Its positive scope simultaneously supports keeping static contracts as the default core, which my position preserves.

Violating LLVM no-alias promises is UB — an unverifiable static assertion at a boundary risks silent miscompilation, whereas a guard failure deoptimizes safely. Also shows LLVM has explicit fact-encoding machinery that guard-verified facts could feed (with a soundness obligation I list as a gap).

Enumerates the optimization classes (register promotion, read/write elimination, reordering) that any proven non-interference fact unlocks — the payoff is the same whether the fact is proven by contract or established by a guard, so speculation regions inherit real optimization value, not a separate weaker tier.

Honest cost accounting I build the 'selective' qualifier on: HotSpot tiering trades code-cache memory and warmup for profile quality. This is evidence that whole-program profile-driven optimization has inherent costs — which is exactly why my position confines speculation to designated boundaries instead of adopting it globally.

Vendor-confirmed limit of runtime analysis (escape analysis eliminates some allocations, never guarantees stack allocation) — supports my concession that speculation yields conditional results, and therefore supports designating regions explicitly so programmers know where guarantees end.

Corroborates J004 at adversarially-verified confidence: managed-runtime recovery is conditional, inlining- and profile-dependent, not a source-level guarantee. I use it to bound my claim: speculation is for facts contracts cannot express, never a replacement for facts they can.

SPARK shows the static-contract route taken to its limit is 'verification-oriented and may not map directly to a general-purpose performance language without usability costs' (negative scope) — evidence that a purely contract-based language faces real coverage/practicality limits at exactly the boundaries where I propose guarded speculation.

concessions: Confidence asymmetry cuts against me: the F-cards supporting static contracts survived 3-0 adversarial verification, while every J-card my case leans on is provisional direct-extraction (ground rule 3). The best-verified evidence in this corpus favors the static-contract side; my case rests partly on provisional cards plus design reasoning the corpus cannot confirm.

J001's negative scope concedes the residual cost of guards versus static knowledge is unquantified — I cannot claim guarded code approaches contract-code performance, only that it beats worst-case-assumption code, and even that comparison is unbenchmarked in this corpus.
J003's negative scope notes real-world performance differences from shape machinery 'are often small' — the magnitude of speculation's wins is unproven in either direction.

Inside speculation regions, the language necessarily loses guarantee-grade performance transparency: results are conditional (J004, F007 — the latter with explicitly LOW design-generalization confidence), data-dependent, and can degrade under polymorphism or shape churn (J003). My position accepts a two-tier transparency model.
Warmup and code-cache/memory costs are inherent to profile-driven machinery (J005); a 'selective' design shrinks but does not eliminate them.

Per caveats.md, the corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection and provides no verified coverage of dynamic runtimes beyond these cards (Julia, JS engines beyond V8 docs, dynamic typing, Mojo, etc.); nothing here settles this debate, and I do not claim it does.
If static contracts turn out to be practical at nearly all real module boundaries (unmeasured — see gaps), my position collapses to a large engineering expenditure for a rarely-used mechanism.
risks: Boundary creep: once deopt machinery exists, pressure grows to designate more regions speculative because it is easier than writing contracts, eroding the statically-analyzable core whose benefits are the corpus's best-verified findings (F001, F002).

One-directional degradation ratchets: J002 documents that V8 element-kind generalization is one-way — a single anomalous input can permanently degrade a region's specialization until recompilation. An optimizer-first language inheriting such ratchets gets performance cliffs that are invisible in source.

Soundness interaction hazard: lowering guard-established dynamic facts onto LLVM's UB-based encodings (F003) is only sound if every invalidation path (including concurrent ones) is proven to reach deopt before any UB-relying code executes; a bug here converts a safe guard failure into silent miscompilation — the exact failure my position claims to prevent.

Deopt machinery is notoriously hard engineering (OSR, frame reconstruction, invalidation under concurrency); the corpus contains no card on its cost or bug rate, so this risk is asserted from general knowledge, not evidence — flagged accordingly.

Performance opacity within regions contradicts the language's founding ethos: programmers reading source cannot predict guarded-region performance without knowing runtime data distributions (J003), which may be judged unacceptable for an optimizer-first, performance-transparent language.

AOT deployment contexts (the likely target for an LLVM-based language) may not tolerate warmup or dual code paths at all (J005 analog), making the mechanism dead weight in the most important deployments.
evidenceGaps: Quantified residual cost of guards versus static knowledge — explicitly unquantified per J001's negative scope; without this number, 'guards are cheap enough' is an assumption. Needed: microbenchmark-grade studies of guard overhead in specialized fast paths.

Zero corpus coverage of AOT+deopt hybrid systems — GraalVM Native Image with PGO, JavaScriptCore FTL, HHVM, Julia's static-then-dynamic model, persisted-profile approaches. caveats.md explicitly lists Julia, JS engines, and dynamic typing as unverified areas. These systems are the closest existing analogs to my proposal; their documented successes/failures would largely decide this debate.
No card on LLVM's own deopt facilities (llvm.experimental.deoptimize, operand bundles, stackmaps/patchpoints); feasibility of sound deopt on the mandated LLVM target is unverified.

Frequency and importance of boundaries where static contracts are genuinely impractical: the corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection (caveats.md), so we cannot estimate what fraction of real optimizer-relevant facts fall outside contract reach. If it is small, my position loses on cost-benefit.

Soundness proof obligation: no evidence on whether guard-established facts can be soundly combined with UB-based IR metadata (F003 negative scope: metadata is only sound if frontend obligations are true and survive lowering) in the presence of invalidation.
Engineering cost and historical bug rate of deoptimization machinery in production compilers — asserted from general knowledge in my risks, not backed by any card.

Cards N001-N006, A001-A010, D001-D010, C001-C006 were listed as citable in my brief but do not appear in any of the five files I was directed to read; my case may be missing relevant evidence for or against my position from those series.

result.position: Static contracts must carry every correctness-critical optimizer fact (aliasing, non-interference, ownership, lifetime/provenance); profile-driven machinery is admitted only for cost decisions — inlining, code/data layout, and speculative devirtualization behind explicit guards — where a wrong or stale profile can cost performance but can never cause a miscompile. No profile-derived fact may ever be lowered into a UB-bearing IR contract (noalias-class attributes/metadata) without a runtime guard that makes the fact locally true.
result.coreArgument:
The principled dividing line is quantification: a fact is correctness-critical when the enabled transform is unsound if the fact is false on ANY execution. LLVM's own contract design makes this concrete — noalias attributes and alias.scope/noalias metadata declare universally-quantified disjointness, and violating them is undefined behavior (F003, F004). A profile is, by construction, a sample of SOME executions on SOME inputs; no sampling regime can establish a universally quantified property. Therefore feeding profile-derived facts into UB-bearing IR contracts is a miscompilation channel by design, not by accident. The only sound way speculation can use an observed-but-unproven fact is behind a guard — and once the guard exists, correctness is established locally by the guard branch, not by the profile; the profile is merely deciding whether the guard is worth its cost. That is exactly my position: contracts define what is sound; profiles select among sound alternatives.

Both halves are the best-supported story the corpus can tell. Half one — correctness facts belong in source contracts: the corpus's strongest, adversarially-verified findings are precisely that language-level non-interference contracts are central optimizer enablers (Rust &mut exclusivity, Fortran dummy-argument rules, SPARK ownership/anti-aliasing — F001), that their payoff is enabling transforms whose soundness spans all executions (register promotion, redundant-load and dead-store elimination, reordering — F002), that SPARK demonstrates these contracts can be mechanically checked rather than merely trusted (F006), and that the contract channel must be preserved into an optimizer-visible IR rather than erased early (F009). Half two — cost facts belong to profiles: hotness, branch bias, call-site type skew, and layout affinity are properties of a WORKLOAD, not of the program; they cannot be truthfully written as universal source contracts (a source-level 'hot' annotation is either unverifiable or wrong when the workload shifts — and the corpus's own caveat discipline notes documentation/contract-style evidence cannot quantify performance magnitudes). The JIT evidence shows this information is real and valuable: runtimes lacking static facts all converge on profiling+guards to recover the same information static types would carry (J001), and monomorphic, shape-stable sites are what actually optimize (J003). But the same evidence shows what it costs to let RUNTIME machinery carry the load: invalidation/deoptimization infrastructure (J002), warmup time and code-cache memory (J005), and results that are conditional on inlining depth and analysis limits rather than guaranteed (F007, J004 — Oracle explicitly documents escape analysis as conditional elimination, not guaranteed stack allocation). Offline PGO with guarded speculation takes the information channel J001 identifies while refusing the runtime machinery J002/J005 price out, and it fits an AOT, LLVM-targeted, performance-transparent language: deopt-for-correctness would import the heaviest part of a JIT, whereas a guard is an ordinary branch with a statically compiled fallback — auditable, costable, and semantics-preserving.

Against the pure-static pole (advocate 1's likely position): refusing profiles does not make cost facts static; it makes them guesses. Fortran-style unchecked assumption of programmer claims is the cautionary tale the corpus itself flags — restrict-like contracts 'depend on programmer truthfulness' (F001 negative scope) — and extending that lie-prone channel to workload claims (hotness, likely-target) adds risk with no soundness payoff, since cost facts never needed soundness in the first place. Against the runtime-speculation pole: F007/J004/J005 document conditionality, warmup, and memory costs, and performance transparency dies if what the compiler may ASSUME depends on what a profiler happened to observe. My split gives each fact class its correct channel: what may be assumed is readable in source and checked; what is likely is an explicit, versioned build input.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Workflow-confirmed: LLVM noalias attributes and alias.scope/noalias metadata make violation undefined behavior, and the negative scope states the metadata is only sound if frontend obligations are true. This is the load-bearing soundness argument: correctness facts lowered to LLVM are universal promises, so they must originate from source contracts (or guards), never from sampled profiles.

noalias returns are allocation-strength contracts, and the negative scope says source languages need lifetime/provenance/escape rules before emitting them — i.e., the strongest optimizer facts structurally require source-level semantic rules, which no profile can substitute for.

The corpus's central confirmed finding: language-level non-interference contracts (Rust exclusivity, Fortran dummy args, SPARK anti-aliasing) are what enable otherwise-unsound optimizations. Supports the 'static correctness' half: the proven enabler class is source contracts, not runtime observation.

The payoff of alias facts is register promotion, read/write elimination, and reordering — transforms whose soundness requires non-interference on every execution path crossing the write. This shows why correctness facts are universally quantified and hence profile-inadmissible.

SPARK shows a checked route to contracts exists (ownership transfer, anti-aliasing, side-effect-free expressions), answering the objection that source contracts must be blindly trusted: the correctness channel can be verified, unlike a profile.

Swift SIL shows performance-critical semantics should stay optimizer-visible in the IR pipeline. Supports my architecture: the contract channel runs source-to-IR; the profile channel attaches only cost weights and guard placement, never semantic facts.

Workflow-confirmed and explicitly low design-generalization: HotSpot escape analysis yields conditional elimination, not guaranteed stack allocation, dependent on inlining/profile/context. Argues against leaving guarantee-class facts to runtime machinery — runtime recovery is best-effort where contracts are definitional.

(Provisional) Primary-vendor sharpening of F007: Oracle states escape analysis does not replace heap with stack allocation. Reinforces that runtime analysis delivers conditional wins, so a performance-transparent language cannot delegate correctness- or guarantee-critical facts to it.

(Provisional) CPython/V8/PyPy converge on profiling+guards to recover exactly the information static languages state in types — same information demand, different channel. Supports my split two ways: state statically what can be stated; keep profiling for the residue (hotness, site skew) that types cannot state. Its negative scope also honestly flags my unquantified guard-cost gap.

(Provisional) Speculation requires invalidation machinery (de-specialization counters, deopt marking, one-directional generalization) — the infrastructure cost my position avoids by using plain guards with compiled fallbacks instead of deoptimization. Its negative scope ('deopt also enables optimizations static compilers cannot legally do without profiles') is simultaneously the steelman for admitting profile-steered guarded speculation at all, which my position does.

(Provisional) HotSpot tiering trades code-cache memory and warmup for profile quality — concrete evidence that in-runtime profiling has inherent runtime costs. Offline PGO moves those costs to build time, preserving runtime performance transparency.

(Provisional) Monomorphism and shape stability are the optimizer-relevant properties in V8. In a static language, layout stability is guaranteed by contract; WHICH call sites are monomorphic on a real workload is irreducibly a profile fact — the exact division of labor my position codifies (contract guarantees the shape; profile picks devirt candidates behind guards).

Fortran shows compilers can assume unchecked contracts — and its POINTER/TARGET/coarray exceptions plus dependence on programmer truthfulness (with F001's negative scope) show the lie-channel risk of unchecked assertions. Supports preferring checked contracts for correctness facts and refusing to add unverifiable workload assertions to the same trusted channel.

concessions: J002's negative scope is a genuine point for the JIT side: deoptimization enables transforms an AOT-plus-guards design can never do (guard-free speculation, invalidated wholesale on assumption failure). My position permanently forgoes that headroom, and the corpus does not quantify how much headroom that is.

The residual cost of guards versus true static knowledge is explicitly unquantified (J001 negative scope). I cannot show from this corpus that guarded speculative devirt approaches statically-devirtualized performance.
All J cards are provisional direct-extraction, not adversarially verified; the runtime-machinery cost side of my argument rests on weaker evidence than the contract side (F cards).

The managed-runtime cost evidence is essentially single-implementation (HotSpot; F007 carries low design-generalization confidence, J004/J005 are version-specific). Other runtimes may have materially different cost profiles.

Unchecked contracts depend on programmer truthfulness (F001/F005 negative scopes), so my 'static correctness' half is only as sound as its checking story; SPARK's checked route may not transfer to a general performance language without usability costs SPARK's own card flags (F006 negative scope) — and this language accepts ergonomic pain, but the feasibility of full checking is still unproven.

The corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection (caveats.md, misleading-comparisons #1); my claim that the correctness-vs-cost split cleanly generalizes to dispatch, layout, numerics, and concurrency is extrapolation beyond confirmed evidence.
risks: Two-input builds (source + profile) introduce profile staleness, cross-build performance non-reproducibility, and performance regressions invisible in source review — a partial erosion of the performance-transparency goal, mitigated but not eliminated by the rule that profiles can never change semantics.

Boundary erosion in practice: 'this pointer never aliased in production' is a standing temptation to promote profile observations into noalias-class metadata without guards — exactly the miscompile channel F003 identifies. The prohibition must be enforced architecturally (e.g., type-separated fact channels in the compiler), not by convention.

Compiler-inserted guards are hidden branches in a language that promises cost transparency; without surfacing guard placement to the programmer (reports or mandatory annotations), the design contradicts its own transparency principle.
PGO can mask missing contracts: teams may recover performance via profiles instead of writing/strengthening contracts, weakening the ecosystem pressure that makes the contract channel rich.

Phase-changing or multi-modal workloads can make any single offline profile misleading for layout and inlining decisions; a JIT adapts where offline PGO cannot, and the corpus gives no evidence on how damaging stale profiles are.
evidenceGaps: No cards exist on AOT profile-guided optimization systems (LLVM instrumented PGO, sampling/AutoFDO, post-link layout tools): the empirical benefit of profile-steered inlining and layout in an AOT setting — the value half of my position — is currently supported only by analogy to JIT evidence (J001/J003/J005) from a different execution model. This is the single most important gap to close.
No cards on speculative devirtualization with guards in AOT compilers (e.g., GCC/LLVM whole-program devirt with speculative fallback) — my central admitted mechanism is entirely uncarded.
Residual guard cost versus static knowledge is unquantified (flagged in J001's negative scope); needed to bound how much my position loses to both the pure-static and full-JIT alternatives.
No evidence on profile staleness/workload-shift harm magnitude, needed to assess the reproducibility and transparency risks above.

The corpus lacks confirmed coverage of data layout, dispatch, numerics, and concurrency (caveats.md), where the correctness-vs-cost classification of facts must be tested case by case before the split can be declared complete.

No evidence on whether a checked-contract surface (SPARK/Rust-style) can cover the full inventory of correctness-critical facts a performance language needs without routing significant code through an unchecked/unsafe assertion channel — if most real code needs unchecked assertions, the 'static correctness' half weakens toward Fortran-style trust (F001/F005 negative scopes).

result.position: Adopt fully explicit per-type/per-operation integer-overflow modes — wrapping, saturating, checked, and unchecked-assume-no-overflow as distinct types/operators with distinct lowerings — and make FP strictness and fast-math permissions explicit scoped annotations. No implicit numeric semantics anywhere: every optimizer license in the emitted IR must correspond to a token the programmer wrote.
result.coreArgument: The language is optimizer-first and LLVM-targeted, and the best-verified evidence in this corpus (N001–N006, all adversarially source-checked, high source-accuracy) shows that LLVM's numeric optimization power is delivered through per-operation, opt-in contracts, not a global mode. Unflagged `add` wraps modulo bit width; adding `nuw`/`nsw` makes overflow poison — the corpus itself states "overflow facts are contracts, not mere annotations" (N002). The undefinedness lattice (N001) shows optimizer license is graded per value/operation, and semantic fuzziness (undef-style per-use nondeterminism) breaks even basic algebraic rewrites — precision of semantics is itself an optimizer asset. Immediate-UB operations like division by zero actively BLOCK speculation while poison-based contracts permit it (N003), so the wrapping/checked/assume choice has concrete, operation-local optimization consequences that a programmer of an optimizer-first language should be able to read directly off the source. InstCombine's validation discipline is organized around exactly this granularity: flag-sensitive folds must be tested and Alive2-proved per flag combination, with fast-math flags handled with analogous care (N006) — per-operation flags are how the optimizer's correctness is actually policed, so a source design isomorphic to them inherits a mature validation story. An explicit per-type/per-operation design is the unique design that is structurally isomorphic to this contract system, and it is the only one that satisfies the corpus's standing soundness rule by construction: flags and metadata are contracts a frontend may emit only when source semantics make them true (implementation/numeric-semantics-and-poison.md; misleading-comparisons #5 "IR mechanisms are conditional carriers of source facts"; F003 states the same conditionality for noalias metadata). Every competitor fails one side: an implicit UB-on-overflow default (C-style) emits nsw with no source-level license, maximizing deferred-UB risk that N001/N002 show is real and propagates subtly; an implicit checked default inserts branches whose cost is invisible in source, violating performance transparency; an implicit wrapping default (Java-style) silently forfeits the nsw/nuw license everywhere and hardwires an anti-optimization invariant into all code that never asked for it. Distinct source types/operators also preserve programmer intent into the IR pipeline, which F009 supports as an optimization enabler (IRs that keep source-level semantics long enough to optimize them, as Swift SIL does). Finally, the corpus explicitly lists the overflow-default and strict-vs-fast-FP questions as undecided and under-evidenced ("Still undecided" in the numeric-semantics note): "nothing implicit" is the design that defers the least-evidenced decision (which default wins) while shipping the well-evidenced machinery (per-operation contracts). For FP specifically, the verified corpus is nearly silent — every N-card's negative scope excludes strict-vs-fast, FMA/contraction, and reproducibility — so no default can honestly be claimed superior; the conservative position consistent with the evidence is that value-changing FP transforms are permissions the programmer grants explicitly, in a bounded scope, lowering to per-instruction fast-math flags whose per-flag canonicalization discipline N006 verifies exists. Ergonomic verbosity is an accepted cost under this language's charter; unsoundness is not, and this is the only proposal in the debate whose soundness does not depend on a default guess the corpus says we cannot yet justify.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Cornerstone: verifies LLVM already distinguishes wrapping (unflagged add, modulo 2^n) from assume-no-overflow (nuw/nsw producing poison) as distinct per-instruction contracts, and frames overflow facts as contracts. My proposal makes the source language isomorphic to this: wrapping and unchecked-assume-no-overflow are distinct operators with one-to-one, transparent lowerings.

The verified undefinedness lattice (immediate UB > poison > undef > freeze(poison) > concrete) shows optimizer license is graded and per-value, and that semantic imprecision (undef per-use nondeterminism) invalidates ordinary rewrites like x*2 -> x+x. Supports the claim that precise, explicitly chosen per-operation semantics are themselves an optimization enabler, and that granting license should be deliberate because poison-based UB is deferred and propagating.

Division by zero is immediate UB and demonstrably blocks speculation (udiv cannot be hoisted above its guard). This proves mode choice has operation-local optimization consequences in both directions — some modes grant license, some revoke it — which is exactly the information a performance-transparent language should surface per operation, and it motivates keeping a checked/trapping form semantically distinct from a UB form.

InstCombine's contributor discipline is organized per poison-generating flag: flag-sensitive folds need dedicated tests, negative tests, and Alive2 proofs, 'with analogous care for fast-math flags.' This supports (a) per-operation flag granularity is how the optimizer is actually validated, so a source design at that granularity inherits the validation story, and (b) the only verified corpus anchor that scoped FP fast-math permissions have a per-instruction lowering target with an existing correctness discipline. (Note: medium confidence card; one constituent claim was 2-1.)

Range metadata is a per-value semantic promise with poison consequences, verified as an optimizer-facing fact channel rather than a hint. Supports the per-TYPE half of my position by showing LLVM can consume type-shaped numeric promises (e.g., future range-restricted integer types) — flagged as extrapolation beyond the card's literal scope.

States (for noalias/alias.scope) the general principle that LLVM optimizer-facing facts are sound only if frontend obligations are actually true. My 'nothing implicit' rule operationalizes this: an nsw or fast-math flag may only be emitted because the programmer wrote an explicit assume-no-overflow operator or scoped annotation, so every emitted contract has a visible source-level license.

Verified finding that optimization-first design benefits from IRs preserving source-language semantics long enough to optimize them (Swift SIL). Distinct overflow-mode types/operators preserve numeric intent from source into the pipeline instead of erasing it into a single default, supporting the per-type/per-operator encoding over an ambient mode. (Medium generalization confidence.)

concessions: Every N-card carries design_generalization_confidence: LOW, and the corpus explicitly states no source-language numeric policy is selected. My argument is an inference from verified IR contracts to source design; the corpus does not verify that inference itself.

Two of my four modes have zero corpus evidence: no card covers LLVM's checked-overflow intrinsics (llvm.*.with.overflow) or saturating intrinsics (llvm.*.sat), their existence, or their optimizer treatment. That half of the proposal rests on unverified background knowledge.

The FP half of my position is the weakest: every N-card's negative scope explicitly excludes strict-vs-fast FP, FMA/contraction, rounding modes, reproducibility, and GPU math. I have one clause of N006 (fast-math flags get analogous per-flag care) plus analogy — not a verified FP semantics model.

No quantified performance evidence exists anywhere in the corpus (caveats.md: documentation evidence 'usually does not quantify performance magnitude'). I cannot prove nsw/nuw or fast-math delivers measurable speedups, nor bound the cost of checked mode, so 'performance transparency' currently means transparent CONTRACTS, not measured costs.

'Nothing implicit' is itself an unevidenced human-factors bet: the corpus contains no data on whether explicit modes reduce or merely relocate programmer error, and forced verbosity may drive convention-level pseudo-defaults that recreate implicitness without its consistency.

An opponent arguing for a safe implicit default (e.g., checked-by-default with explicit escape hatches) can claim equal soundness with better ergonomics; my counterargument (invisible cost violates performance transparency) is a charter appeal, not an evidence-backed refutation.
risks: Deferred-UB debuggability: explicit unchecked operators emit poison-producing flags, and N001/N002 show poison propagates and surfaces far from its origin; wide availability of a cheap-looking unchecked operator may sprinkle latent UB through performance-chasing code (cargo-culting risk), and the corpus offers no evidence on mitigations such as unsafe-gating.

Type-system combinatorics: encoding modes as distinct types forces generic code to be polymorphic over overflow mode or duplicated per mode, and requires mixed-mode expression rules (wrapping + checked = ?) that the corpus says nothing about; this complexity could itself introduce soundness bugs in the frontend.

Scoped fast-math annotations may not compose soundly across inlining and cross-function optimization; if annotation scope is not faithfully preserved through the pipeline (the F009/misleading-#5 preservation concern), a locally granted permission could leak into code that never granted it — a soundness violation, not just a quality regression.

Perverse incentive: if checked mode's branch density inhibits vectorization/speculation (plausible from N003's guard-blocking pattern but unquantified), the 'safe' mode may be uncompetitive enough that users reflexively reach for unchecked, making the explicit design safety-theater in practice.

Canonicalization friction: InstCombine canonical forms are defined over specific flag combinations (N006); heavily mixed per-operation modes in one expression tree may block folds that a uniform-default language would get, potentially costing performance exactly where the design promises it.
evidenceGaps: Verified cards on LLVM checked-overflow (llvm.*.with.overflow) and saturating (llvm.*.sat) intrinsics: their semantics, optimizer support (branch folding, vectorization of overflow checks), and codegen cost — required before 'checked' and 'saturating' can be claimed to have efficient first-class lowerings.

Verified cards on individual fast-math flags (nnan, ninf, reassoc, contract, afn), constrained-FP/strictfp intrinsics, and — critically — how fast-math flags behave across inlining and interprocedural passes; without this, the soundness of SCOPED FP permissions cannot be established.

Quantified benchmarks: overhead of checked arithmetic on real workloads, measured benefit of nsw/nuw and fast-math on optimization outcomes, and vectorization impact of per-operation mode mixing; the corpus contains zero performance numbers.

Comparative language evidence for per-operation mode designs (Zig's +%/std wrap-sat operators, Rust's wrapping_/checked_/overflowing_ APIs and Wrapping<T>, Swift's &+, C#'s checked{} blocks, Ada's range types): the A/D/C/J direct-extraction cards were not among my assigned files, so I could not cite them; note they are provisional (not adversarially verified) even where they exist.
Human-factors evidence: error/misuse rates for explicit unchecked operators, whether unsafe-gating reduces misuse, and whether 'no default' produces convention-level defaults in practice.
Per-type vs per-operator encoding trade-offs for generic code, type inference, and mixed-mode expression semantics — no corpus coverage at all.

FP reproducibility, rounding modes, FP exceptions, and GPU/approximate math are explicitly listed as undecided in the corpus; my position's FP half cannot win the debate until at least strict-mode lowering evidence exists.

result.position: Adopt type-enforced data-race freedom: Send/Sync-like sharing capabilities plus exclusive borrowing in the type system, so data races are unrepresentable in safe code, non-atomic memory is statically provably unshared, and racy semantics (UB or otherwise) only ever need to be defined for the explicitly-unsafe/FFI subset.
result.coreArgument:
The debate over race semantics is usually framed as a two-way tradeoff — UB races (C/C++/Rust/Swift) buy maximal optimizer freedom at the cost of unbounded blast radius, while bounded races (Java/Go/JS) cap the blast radius but permanently tax the optimizer (C002). Type-enforced DRF dissolves that dilemma rather than picking a side: if the type system makes races unrepresentable in safe code, the optimizer gets the strong side of the tradeoff (it may assume no races on all non-atomic memory) without the language ever exposing safe programmers to UB. This is not speculative — C004 documents that Rust already demonstrates exactly this route: Send/Sync auto traits plus exclusive &mut borrowing prevent data races in safe code, "so the optimizer can treat non-atomic memory as unshared unless types say otherwise."

Why this matters specifically for an OPTIMIZER-FIRST language: C003 enumerates the concrete single-thread optimizations that die when memory *might* be shared — the compiler must not introduce writes on paths that would not store, must not let one read observe multiple values by re-loading, must not cache values in registers across iterations, and must treat fences as motion barriers. Under bounded-race semantics these restrictions apply pervasively, because almost any memory might be shared. Under type-enforced DRF, the set of memory subject to those restrictions is exactly the set the types mark as shared/atomic; everything else is provably thread-private or exclusively borrowed, and the optimizer keeps full sequential freedom over it. F001 and F002 independently confirm (workflow-confirmed, high source accuracy) that language-level non-aliasing/non-interference guarantees — Rust's safe &mut exclusivity foremost — are the central optimizer enablers, and that their value is precisely proving non-interference around writes. Type-level sharing facts are the concurrency-side completion of the same principle: exclusivity in space (aliasing) and exclusivity across threads (Send/Sync) are one mechanism.

The lowering story also favors this position. LLVM already provides optimizer-facing encodings for disjointness (noalias attributes, alias.scope/noalias metadata, F003) and a graded atomics vocabulary from NotAtomic to SequentiallyConsistent with pass-by-pass transformation legality (C005). F003's own caveat — that this metadata is sound only if the frontend's obligations are actually true — is the strongest argument FOR type enforcement: restrict-style programmer promises depend on programmer truthfulness (F001 scope limits), whereas borrow-checked exclusivity turns the frontend obligation into a theorem. An optimizer-first language should want its noalias facts to be proofs, not pinky-swears. Meanwhile C001 shows every major language converges on DRF-SC as the baseline contract; type-enforced DRF makes safe code satisfy the DRF precondition by construction, so safe programmers get sequential consistency as a theorem and never reason about weak behaviors.

Finally, this position fits the language's stated priorities exactly. The known cost of Send/Sync + borrow checking is ergonomic friction and verbosity — which this language explicitly permits ("possibly ugly/verbose"). What the language may NOT sacrifice is soundness — and among the three candidate models, only type-enforced DRF makes race-freedom of safe code a static guarantee rather than a programmer obligation (UB model) or a runtime-bounded hope (bounded model). Explicit, even ugly, capability annotations (thread-transfer capability, share capability, explicit atomic types with explicit orderings mapping 1:1 onto C005's gradation) are performance-transparent in precisely the way the language wants: the type signature tells you which optimizer regime a value lives in.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: The existence proof for my position: Rust's Send/Sync auto traits plus exclusive &mut borrowing prevent data races in safe code, and the card states the direct optimizer consequence — non-atomic memory can be treated as unshared unless types say otherwise. This is the exact mechanism I advocate, demonstrated in a shipping systems language.

Quantifies (qualitatively) what is at stake: possibly-shared memory forbids introducing writes, re-loading a value into one read, register-caching across iterations, and code motion across fences. Type-enforced non-sharing confines all of these restrictions to the explicitly-shared subset, recovering full single-thread freedom elsewhere — the core optimizer payoff of my position.

Documents the UB-vs-bounded tradeoff my position escapes: UB races give optimizers maximal freedom but unbounded consequences; bounded races (Java/Go) constrain optimizations. Type enforcement gets UB-tier optimizer assumptions in safe code without exposing safe programmers to UB, because racy safe programs cannot be written.

DRF-SC is the convergent baseline contract across C, C++, Java, JS, Rust, Swift, Go. Under my position, safe code satisfies the data-race-free precondition by construction, so DRF-SC's strong conclusion (sequentially consistent behavior) holds as a theorem for all safe code — the simplest possible programmer-facing model.

Workflow-confirmed (6 sources, high accuracy): language-level non-aliasing/non-interference rules — with Rust's safe &mut exclusivity as the lead example — are central optimizer enablers. Exclusive borrowing is the same mechanism that delivers thread-level non-interference; my position extends an already-confirmed enabler rather than adding a new unproven one.

Confirms the optimization value of alias information is specifically proving non-interference around writes. Cross-thread interference is the worst-case interference; a type system that proves memory is unshared proves the strongest form of write non-interference the optimizer can use.

LLVM noalias/alias.scope metadata exists to carry disjointness to the optimizer, but is sound only if frontend obligations are true. This is my strongest lowering argument: type-checked exclusivity makes those obligations compiler-verified theorems, unlike restrict-style programmer assertions (whose truthfulness-dependence F001's scope limits flag).

LLVM's graded atomic orderings (NotAtomic → SequentiallyConsistent), each with specific transformation legality, give the target vocabulary: my model lowers provably-unshared memory to plain NotAtomic accesses with full pass freedom, and explicitly-shared memory to the exact ordering the source type declares — a clean, performance-transparent 1:1 mapping.

Secondary support: SPARK shows a second independent language achieving optimizer-friendly semantics by restricting mutable aliasing and imposing ownership/transfer policies on pointer-like types — corroborating that ownership-style static restriction is a viable, not Rust-idiosyncratic, design route (with acknowledged usability costs, which this language tolerates).

concessions: The guarantee is conditional on a trusted unsafe boundary: C004's negative scope is explicit that unsafe code and incorrect manual Send/Sync implementations are UB. 'Races are unrepresentable' is true only of safe code; the language still must define semantics for races that unsafe code can create, and one wrong manual Sync capability implementation silently reintroduces UB with full blast radius.

Type enforcement does not eliminate memory-model design work: C004's negative scope notes an atomics model is still required for the intentionally-shared subset, and C005/C003 show source ordering semantics and IR semantics differ (LLVM treats racing non-atomic loads as undef, not instant UB) — the safe/unsafe boundary semantics must be nailed down against LLVM's actual contract.

The claim that exclusivity lowers cleanly to noalias everywhere is flagged as misleading in the corpus (misleading-comparisons.jsonl entry 2): unsafe code, interior mutability, raw pointers, shared references, and callbacks complicate lowering. My position needs a principled interior-mutability primitive (an UnsafeCell analogue), and that primitive punches real holes in the 'provably unshared' story that must be tracked in the type system.

No performance quantification exists anywhere in the corpus: C002's negative scope, F002's limits, and caveats.md all state that documentation-grounded evidence shows what languages claim, not measured speedups. 'Full single-thread optimizer freedom' is a qualitative claim about preserved transformation legality, not a benchmarked delta over bounded-race semantics.

The corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection bias (caveats.md, misleading-comparisons.jsonl entry 1), which structurally flatters my position — the well-attested evidence happens to be exactly the evidence my side cites. That is a reason for the decision process to discount, not dismiss, the apparent one-sidedness.

All C-cards (C001–C006) are direct-extraction/provisional per the ground rules and lean on secondary sources (the Swtch memory-model survey, the JSR-133 FAQ, the Nomicon — which caveats.md notes is explanatory, not a complete formal aliasing model). Primary sources (C++ standard, JLS chapter 17) were not fetched (implementation/concurrency-atomics-memory-model.md, 'Open').
risks: Soundness concentration risk: the entire optimizer contract rests on the unsafe boundary being small and correct. Every lock-free structure, allocator, and FFI shim gets pushed into unsafe code where the language's guarantees vanish; if the ecosystem's unsafe layer is buggy, the optimizer's 'provably unshared' assumption becomes a miscompilation amplifier, exactly the UB blast radius C002 attributes to the C/C++ model.

Formalization risk: caveats.md notes Rust's aliasing model is not fully formalized (Nomicon is explanatory; future aliasing-model changes are an open caveat). Building an optimizer-first language on exclusivity-derived noalias facts before the source-level aliasing model is precisely specified risks the same decade of frontend/LLVM contract mismatches Rust experienced (specific rustc mutable-noalias history is NOT in the corpus — flagged as a gap, not asserted as evidence).

Foreign-world risk: foreign threads, callbacks, signal handlers, and finalizers (open questions in implementation/concurrency-atomics-memory-model.md) sit outside the type system's reach; an FFI-heavy target domain could hollow out the guarantee in practice.

Expressiveness/adoption risk: F006's scope limits warn that SPARK-style restriction 'may not map directly to a general-purpose performance language without usability costs.' The mandate tolerates ergonomic pain, but if legitimate shared-mutable patterns are so painful that programmers route everything through the escape hatch, the effective guarantee degrades to the UB model with extra steps.

Opportunity-cost risk: the corpus contains no confirmed evidence about the bounded-race alternative's actual optimization cost (C002 negative scope). If that cost turned out to be small in practice, this position would be paying substantial type-system complexity for a performance delta that was never measured.
evidenceGaps: No quantified performance comparison between the three regimes (UB races vs bounded races vs type-enforced DRF) — C002's negative scope and caveats.md both say the corpus cannot quantify magnitude. A benchmark study (e.g., Java/Go-style bounded semantics imposed on otherwise-identical code vs race-free-assumed code through LLVM) is the single most decision-relevant missing artifact.

No verified evidence on how reliably exclusive-borrow facts survive lowering to LLVM noalias/alias.scope in practice (rustc's mutable-noalias enable/disable history and associated LLVM miscompiles would be the key case study; not present in corpus). Without it, 'preserving full single-thread optimizer freedom' is a legality claim, not a demonstrated-pipeline claim.

No formal-soundness evidence for Send/Sync-style capability systems (e.g., RustBelt-class machine-checked proofs) in the corpus; the position asserts 'races unrepresentable in safe code' on documentation authority (C004, Nomicon) only.

Primary memory-model sources missing: C++ standard text and JLS chapter 17 fetches were blocked; Swift, Ada, Pony, and Erlang models and GPU memory models are uncovered (implementation/concurrency-atomics-memory-model.md 'Open'). Pony in particular matters because it is the strongest known alternative type-enforced design point.

Interior mutability is uncovered: no cards address the design or optimizer cost of the UnsafeCell-analogue that any Send/Sync system requires, even though it is the main hole in 'non-atomic memory is provably unshared.'
No evidence on compile-time cost, annotation burden at scale, or expressiveness ceiling of borrow-checking + capability inference for an optimizer-first (rather than safety-first) language.

The ground rules reference card series N001–N006, A001–A010, D001–D010, and J001–J005, but none of those cards appear in the five files I was directed to read; only F001–F009 and C001–C006 were available to cite. If those series exist elsewhere in the corpus, this case is argued from an incomplete card set.

result.position: Trap-by-default: integer overflow traps deterministically in all build modes (Ada direction); floating point is strict IEEE by default; every relaxation (wrapping, unchecked/poison arithmetic, fast-math, FMA contraction) is an explicit, locally-scoped opt-in that is visible in source and maps to per-operation IR contracts.
result.coreArgument: For an optimizer-first language where soundness may not be sacrificed, trap-by-default is the only overflow default that simultaneously satisfies both constraints the corpus actually documents. First, the corpus establishes that LLVM's overflow machinery is a system of contracts, not hints: unflagged `add` wraps modulo 2^n, while `nuw`/`nsw` turn overflow into poison (N002), and poison sits inside an explicit definedness lattice where transforms must move toward more-defined semantics (N001). The corpus's own implementation warning is that a frontend may emit these contracts only when 'source semantics, verifier, runtime checks, or unsafe boundary make the promises true' (implementation/numeric-semantics-and-poison.md). Trap-by-default is precisely the 'runtime checks' route made systematic: after a deterministic trap check, overflow provably cannot occur on the surviving path, so the frontend can emit `nsw`/`nuw` (N002) and `range` facts (N004) that are true by construction rather than by programmer hope. The competing defaults each fail one horn: UB-by-default (C direction) hands the optimizer maximal freedom but makes every latent-overflow program meaningless at the source level, violating the soundness ground rule — and N001 shows even LLVM internally treats unconstrained nondeterminism as costly (raw `undef` is deprecated because per-use nondeterminism invalidates rewrites as simple as x*2 → x+x). Wrap-by-default (Java direction) is sound but forfeits the overflow contracts entirely: the optimizer must preserve modulo semantics everywhere (N002), and no relaxation fact is ever recoverable without programmer annotation. Trap-by-default keeps totality AND generates optimizer facts. Second, deterministic-in-all-builds semantics gives a precise refinement target: LLVM's own canonicalization culture expects flag-sensitive folds to be Alive2-proved against defined source/target semantics (N006); a language whose default numeric semantics is 'correct value or trap' is translation-validatable in a way that 'anything may happen' is not. Third, the cost shape of traps is one LLVM already engineers around: integer division by zero is immediate UB that blocks speculation above guards (N003), so 'checks constrain hoisting' is already baseline for div/rem in every candidate design — trap-by-default extends a known cost model rather than introducing a novel one. Fourth, on FP: strict IEEE by default with per-scope opt-in relaxation is the design that structurally matches LLVM's native relaxation granularity, which is per-operation flags and metadata (N002, N004, N006 — the InstCombine guide handles fast-math flags with the same poison-flag testing discipline as nuw/nsw), not a global mode; and the undef lesson (N001) is direct evidence that value-nondeterminism — which fast-math-by-default injects into every FP expression — degrades even the optimizer's own ability to do algebraic reasoning consistently. An optimizer-first language can afford verbose opt-ins ('possibly ugly' is in the charter); it cannot afford defaults whose enabling promises are unverifiable. Trap-by-default is the unique default where every emitted IR contract is discharged by construction, which is exactly what the corpus's standing evidence discipline demands.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Establishes that LLVM separates default arithmetic results (unflagged add wraps modulo 2^n) from optimization promises (nsw/nuw make overflow poison) and states overflow facts are 'contracts, not mere annotations.' This is the load-bearing fact for my position: trap checks are the mechanism that makes emitting nsw/nuw sound by construction on post-check paths, so trap-by-default converts a safety feature into a lawful optimizer-fact generator, whereas wrap-by-default forfeits these contracts and UB-by-default makes them promises nobody verified.

Documents LLVM's definedness lattice (immediate UB > poison > undef > freeze > concrete) and that raw undef is deprecated because per-use nondeterminism invalidates ordinary rewrites like x*2 → x+x. This cuts two ways in my favor: (a) it shows unconstrained nondeterminism is costly even to the optimizer itself, undermining the claim that maximal undefinedness maximizes optimization — directly relevant against UB-by-default integers and fast-math-by-default FP; (b) it shows valid transforms must move toward more-defined semantics, so a maximally-defined source default (trap) is the safe top of the refinement order from which explicit relaxations descend.

Integer division by zero is already immediate UB in LLVM and the UB manual explicitly rejects hoisting udiv above a guarding branch. This shows (a) the 'guards block speculation' cost that opponents will charge against pervasive trap checks is already present in the baseline for div/rem under every candidate semantics, and (b) LLVM has established, documented rules for optimizing around guard-protected side-effecting numeric operations — the trap model extends a cost shape LLVM already handles rather than inventing a new one.

range metadata is a semantic promise with poison consequences for out-of-range values, not a profiling hint. Trap-by-default is the default under which range facts are cheaply and soundly derivable (post-check values are provably in range), letting the frontend feed a verified numeric-fact channel to the optimizer. It also grounds one of my stated risks: emitting a wrong range fact is worse than emitting none, so check-elision machinery must be verified.

InstCombine treats poison-generating flags (nuw/nsw) and fast-math flags under the same discipline: flag-sensitive transforms need tests and Alive2 source/target refinement proofs. This supports two claims: (a) deterministic source semantics gives a precise @src for translation validation, which an UB-default language lacks for overflowing programs; (b) LLVM's native granularity for numeric relaxation is per-operation flags, which structurally matches 'explicit opt-in with visible scope' rather than a global fast-math default. Noting this card is medium-confidence (one 2-1 vote).

Supporting, not load-bearing: LLVM optimizer-facing encodings (noalias attributes/metadata) carry UB consequences when violated, and the card's scope note says these are sound only if frontend obligations are true and that 'UB consequences belong to IR contract, not necessarily desired source semantics.' This is direct corpus support for my architectural split: keep UB in the IR contract layer where it belongs, discharge it via runtime checks at the source layer — which is exactly what trap-by-default does for overflow.

Grounds a risk I concede rather than a benefit: representation of exceptional control flow measurably affects optimization quality (landingpad vs funclets), and two overbroad EH claims were refuted. Deterministic traps must pick a lowering (abort, unwind, side-exit) and that choice interacts with EH representation; the corpus warns this area is easy to over-claim, so trap lowering needs its own evidence phase.

concessions: The single biggest question — the runtime cost of always-on overflow traps (branch density, inhibited vectorization/reassociation, code size) versus wrap or UB defaults — has zero cards in this corpus. I cannot claim the cost is small; I can only claim the semantics are sound and contract-generating. If the cost is empirically large and unrecoverable by check elision, my position weakens substantially.

The 'Ada direction' label is rhetorical, not evidenced: the corpus's Ada/SPARK cards (F003, F006) cover aliasing and ownership only. There is no verified card on Ada's overflow-check semantics, its optimizer impact, or field experience. That lineage claim is an evidence gap, not evidence.

N003's speculation-blocking evidence covers only integer div/rem. Extrapolating 'LLVM handles guard costs fine' from division to every add/sub/mul in a program is an inference of mine, not a corpus finding; trap density with checked add is orders of magnitude higher than div density.

My FP-strict-by-default plank is the weakest-evidenced part of the position. The corpus explicitly lists strict-vs-fast FP, FMA/contraction, rounding modes, NaN/signed-zero/infinity handling, and reproducibility as 'still undecided,' and every N-card's negative scope disclaims FP generalization. My FP argument rests on architectural symmetry (per-op opt-in matches LLVM flag granularity, N006) and the undef-nondeterminism analogy (N001), not on FP-specific verified evidence.

Deterministic traps 'in all builds' deliberately gives up the Rust-style debug-check/release-wrap asymmetry, which exists precisely to buy back release performance. I trade that performance headroom for semantic uniformity and validatability; that is a real cost, not a free win.

Direct-extraction card series A/D/C/J were named in my brief but are not present in the five assigned corpus files, so parts of the debate space (e.g., competitor-language field experience) that those cards may cover are invisible to this argument.
Per caveats.md and the phase-2 notes ('many stalled subagents'), the numeric corpus is narrow and LLVM-centric; no final design decision should be made from it, including in my favor.
risks: Performance-identity risk: if always-on trap checks cost enough on hot numeric kernels, an optimizer-first language with trap defaults reads as self-contradictory to its target users, who will blanket-annotate 'unchecked' everywhere — recreating a UB-by-default ecosystem with worse ergonomics and no verification benefit.

Check-elision arms race: competitiveness depends on the compiler proving ranges to delete traps. That machinery must itself be sound — N004 shows wrong range facts poison downstream values, so an unsound elision pass converts my 'no source UB' guarantee into silent miscompilation, the worst outcome for this design.

Lowering-quality risk: this corpus contains no card on LLVM's overflow intrinsics (e.g., sadd.with.overflow), -ftrapv/UBSan codegen quality, or how well the backend optimizes compare-and-branch trap patterns. If checked-arithmetic patterns pessimize loop transforms in practice, the cost side of the ledger grows in ways no current card bounds.

Trap-semantics entanglement: deterministic traps need a defined observable behavior (precise vs imprecise trap points, ordering with other side effects, interaction with unwinding). F008 plus the two refuted EH claims (caveats.md) show this design region is both optimization-relevant and easy to over-claim; a careless trap-precision spec could quietly forbid reordering the optimizer needs.

Strict-IEEE-default risk: forbidding contraction/reassociation by default may leave large FP performance on the table versus fast-math-defaulting competitors on exactly the numeric workloads an optimizer-first language courts; with no FP cards in the corpus, I cannot bound this cost at all.

Ecosystem-mismatch risk: if most numeric code ends up inside relaxation scopes anyway, the 'visible scope' property degrades into noise and the default stops describing real programs — the design would then need scope-hygiene tooling the corpus says nothing about.
evidenceGaps: Quantified overhead of trap-on-overflow arithmetic across representative workloads (scalar, loop-heavy, vectorizable), including effect on vectorization, unrolling, and code size, versus wrap and versus nsw/nuw-UB baselines. This is the decisive gap; no corpus card touches it.

LLVM-specific evidence on checked-arithmetic lowering: semantics and optimization quality of overflow intrinsics, whether LLVM eliminates redundant/dominated overflow checks, and how trap branches interact with loop passes. Needed before claiming N003's guard-handling generalizes from div to all arithmetic.

Ada and SPARK primary-source cards on overflow checking semantics, elision guarantees, and field performance experience — currently the 'Ada direction' has no verified corpus support (F003/F006 are aliasing-only).

Field-experience cards on Swift (trap-by-default in release), Rust (debug-trap/release-wrap split), and Zig (safe/unsafe build modes): actual bug-detection value, opt-out frequency, and performance deltas. caveats.md explicitly notes Zig and numeric semantics lack verified coverage.

Any FP evidence at all: strict IEEE reproducibility across targets, semantics and poison behavior of LLVM fast-math flags (nnan/ninf/reassoc), FMA contraction defaults in peer compilers, and the measured cost of strict-by-default on FP kernels. Every N-card negative scope flags this as needing follow-up.

Trap-precision semantics: what precise vs imprecise trapping costs the optimizer (reordering freedom, speculation), and how trap delivery should interact with the EH representation findings in F008 without repeating the refuted-EH-claim mistake.
Check-elision soundness: evidence on verified or translation-validated range-analysis-driven check removal (the N006 Alive2 discipline extended to frontend-emitted range facts per N004).

The A001-A010, D001-D010, C001-C006, and J001-J005 card series referenced in the debate brief were not present in the five assigned corpus files; whatever competitor-language or design-precedent evidence they contain must be surfaced before this debate can be scored fairly.

result.position: DRF-SC with bounded racy behavior: data races are program errors, but their outcomes are bounded (no arbitrary undefined behavior), implementations may detect-and-halt (Go/Java direction). The type system stays simpler (no mandatory ownership discipline for sharing), and the optimizer accepts Go-style implementation constraints on possibly-shared memory, recovering full freedom only where non-sharing/non-interference is proven.
result.coreArgument: First, DRF-SC is not the debate — it is the settled baseline. C001 shows C, C++, Java, JavaScript, Rust, Swift, and Go all converge on the DRF-SC guarantee; my position adopts exactly that consensus contract for correct programs, so nothing is lost on race-free code. The only live question, per C002, is what racy programs mean: UB (C/C++/Rust/Swift) versus bounded outcomes (Java/JS/Go). For an optimizer-first language the deciding constraint is the debate's own ground rule: ergonomics may be sacrificed, soundness may not. UB races are the one place where an aggressive optimizer converts a program error into arbitrary, unbounded miscompilation — and the more aggressive the optimizer (which is this language's entire identity), the larger that blast radius grows. Bounded semantics keeps the failure mode 'a wrong value, or a clean halt' (C002: Go explicitly constrains implementations and permits race-detection halts; Java bounds outcomes for security). Detect-and-halt is itself an optimizer-friendly escape hatch: a halting outcome is always a legal refinement, so debug/hardened builds can run full race detection without changing the language's semantics.
