Second, the performance case against bounded races is, on this corpus, unproven: C002's own negative scope states there is no performance quantification of the UB-vs-bounded difference, and the implementation file lists 'empirical cost data for bounded-race semantics' as open. Meanwhile, what the corpus DOES robustly attest as the optimizer enabler is non-interference proofs — F001/F002 (workflow-confirmed, high source accuracy) show the payoff comes from proving a location is not modified/observed by others, and the misleading-comparisons notes explicitly warn against overclaiming beyond 'non-interference is well-attested.' Non-interference is orthogonal to race-UB: C003's list of lost freedoms (no introduced writes, no value-splitting re-loads, no caching shared values in registers, fence motion barriers) applies only to POSSIBLY-shared memory. So the layered design is: bounded semantics as the global soundness floor; escape-analysis, optional Send/Sync-style sharing facts (C004 shows sharing can be a type-level fact without being mandatory everywhere), and explicit noalias-style contracts (F003) to locally re-earn UB-grade freedom where it is actually proven safe — F003's caveat even notes that IR-level UB consequences 'belong to the IR contract, not necessarily desired source semantics,' i.e., we can emit aggressive IR facts locally without making source-level races globally undefined. Third, the target IR is closer to my position than to instant-UB: C005 documents that LLVM already treats racing non-atomic loads as undef rather than instant UB, and grades orderings (NotAtomic→SC) with specific per-level transformation freedoms, giving a concrete, graded lowering vocabulary for bounded shared accesses. Fourth, bounded models still admit carved-out optimizer-exploitable invariants: C006 (Java final fields) shows a bounded-race language can grant publication/initialization guarantees (immutable-after-construction) that optimizers can rely on. The opposing UB position must lean on Rust-style type prevention, but C004's negative scope and the caveats file are explicit that unsafe code, UnsafeCell, raw pointers, and incorrect Send/Sync impls remain UB holes — and an optimizer-first, LLVM-targeted language will have a large unsafe/FFI/foreign-thread surface (the implementation file's own open research questions) where exactly those holes live. Bounded race semantics is the only option on the table whose worst case in that surface is still bounded.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Establishes DRF-SC as the cross-language consensus baseline (C, C++, Java, JS, Rust, Swift, Go), so adopting DRF-SC costs nothing in novelty or correct-program semantics; the debate reduces to racy-program behavior.

Directly documents my position as a proven, shipped design point: Java and Go bound racy outcomes (Go explicitly constraining implementations and allowing detect-and-halt). Its negative scope also states there is NO performance quantification of UB-vs-bounded — so the opposing side's core performance claim is an evidence gap, not evidence.

Honestly prices my position: enumerates the exact optimizer freedoms lost on possibly-shared memory (no introduced writes, no re-loading one read into multiple observed values, no register-caching of shared values, fence motion barriers). Crucially these constraints attach only to memory that MIGHT be shared, which motivates recovering freedom via non-interference proofs rather than via global race-UB.

Shows sharing can be a type-level fact (Send/Sync) that a bounded-race language can adopt optionally to prove non-sharing and unlock full optimization — while its negative scope (unsafe code and wrong Send/Sync impls are UB) is my strongest argument against relying on type prevention as the soundness story: the type guarantee ends at the unsafe/FFI boundary.

LLVM IR already treats racing non-atomic loads as undef rather than instant UB, and provides a graded ordering ladder with per-level transformation legality — a natural, concrete lowering vocabulary for bounded racy semantics on the chosen backend.

Java final-field initialization safety proves bounded-race models can still carve narrow, optimizer-compatible publication guarantees (immutability-after-construction), rebutting the claim that bounded semantics forfeits all exploitable invariants.

Workflow-confirmed (high source accuracy): language-level non-aliasing/non-interference rules are the central optimizer enablers across Rust/Fortran/SPARK — supporting the layered design where optimization power comes from proven non-interference, not from making races UB.

Workflow-confirmed: the optimization value of alias information is specifically proving non-interference around writes — i.e., the well-attested performance lever is orthogonal to the racy-program semantics choice.

LLVM noalias attributes/metadata let the frontend express locally-proven disjointness with IR-level UB on violation; its caveat that IR UB consequences 'belong to the IR contract, not necessarily desired source semantics' shows we can emit aggressive local facts without globally-UB source races.

HotSpot escape analysis shows a bounded-race managed language can conditionally recover allocation/synchronization performance — evidence that Go/Java-direction semantics do not preclude aggressive implementation-level optimization, though the card's low generalization rating means I weight this weakly.

concessions: The optimizer cost is real and permanent on unproven memory: C003's constraints (no introduced writes, no re-loads, no register caching of possibly-shared values) apply by default wherever non-sharing is not proven, and an optimizer-first language will feel this everywhere its analyses fail. I cannot claim the cost is small — no card quantifies it in my favor either.

Precisely specifying 'bounded' racy behavior is known to be hard: the Java memory model's no-out-of-thin-air causality specification is widely regarded as problematic. This is general knowledge, NOT carried by any card in the corpus I read — flagged accordingly, but I will not pretend the concern away.

Guaranteeing per-read bounded values on LLVM likely cannot use plain loads (C005: racing non-atomic loads are undef); shared-capable accesses may need unordered/monotonic atomic lowering, which by C005's gradation forbids some transformations (splitting, narrowing, rematerialization) that plain loads allow. The magnitude of that cost is unquantified in the corpus.

Go's bounded-outcome story is partly load-bearing on its runtime (GC, no wild pointers). In a non-GC systems language, a torn racy read of a multi-word value (fat pointer, slice header) can itself breach memory safety, so 'bounded' requires extra per-type machinery (e.g., single-word tearing limits or access-atomicity rules) that the corpus does not cover.

All C-series cards are direct-extraction and provisional, not adversarially verified; caveats.md is explicit that the verified corpus does not deeply cover concurrency/memory-model topics. My entire case rests on the weaker evidence tier, and I cannot claim otherwise.

The opposing Rust-direction position has genuine strength: C004 shows safe-code data-race freedom is achievable and lets the optimizer treat non-atomic memory as unshared by default — a stronger default than mine. My counter is confined to the unsafe/FFI boundary and to the 'simpler types' design constraint, not a refutation of C004.
risks: Whole-program pessimization by default: without a mandatory ownership/sharing discipline, escape and sharing analyses become load-bearing for performance; where they fail, every access pays C003's constraints, and an optimizer-first language could end up systematically slower than a race-UB competitor on pointer-heavy shared-structure code.

Semantic spec debt: committing to bounded outcomes obligates the language to actually define the bound (tearing granularity, out-of-thin-air exclusion, what detect-and-halt may observe). A vague bound is a soundness hole wearing a safety costume.

Backend mismatch risk: if bounded semantics forces unordered-or-stronger lowering broadly, we inherit C005's per-ordering optimization prohibitions across the heap, and LLVM's pass pipeline (tuned for C/C++-style plain memory) may leave performance on the table in ways hard to recover later.

Interaction hazard with F003-style metadata: noalias/alias.scope facts are IR-UB if violated; emitting them from analyses that assumed race-freedom could reintroduce unbounded miscompilation through the back door in racy programs, defeating the entire point of bounded semantics. Emission rules must be race-robust.

Detect-and-halt is permission, not protection: race detectors have overhead and incomplete coverage (no card covers this), so the practical safety benefit in release builds is only the bounded semantics itself, which is weaker than users may assume.
evidenceGaps: Empirical cost of bounded-race semantics vs UB-race semantics — explicitly listed as open in implementation/concurrency-atomics-memory-model.md and in C002's negative scope. This is THE decisive gap: if the cost is large (say >5-10% on representative shared-memory workloads), my position weakens badly; if small, the opposing position loses its main argument. Needs benchmarks comparing plain vs unordered/monotonic lowering under real pass pipelines.

Primary-source confirmation: C++ standard text and JLS chapter 17 fetches were blocked (implementation file); C001/C002 lean on a secondary survey (research.swtch.com/plmm). The UB-vs-bounded split characterization should be re-verified against primary specs before a decision.

LLVM 'unordered' ordering: whether it is documented as suitable and sufficient for Java/Go-like bounded semantics, and exactly which optimizations it blocks — C005 summarizes the gradation but a dedicated, verified card on unordered's intended use and cost is missing.

Out-of-thin-air: no card covers whether a tractable, formally coherent 'bounded racy outcomes' specification exists (JMM causality problems, promising semantics, etc.). Without this, 'bounded' may be unimplementable as specified.

Torn multi-word values in a non-GC language: no evidence on how Go/Java bounding translates when racy tearing can break memory safety (fat pointers, slices, vtable pairs); need per-type access-atomicity design evidence.

Race-detector feasibility/overhead (TSan-style or Go-race-style) for an LLVM-targeted AOT language: detect-and-halt is cited from Go (C002) but no card covers detector cost, coverage, or interaction with heavy optimization.

Soundness conditions for emitting noalias/alias.scope metadata (F003) from a bounded-race language: which non-interference proofs remain valid in the presence of races, so local IR-UB facts cannot re-create global unbounded behavior.

Card-series availability: the N001-N006, A001-A010, D001-D010, and J001-J005 series referenced in the debate brief were not present in any of the five listed corpus files; my case cites only F and C series. If those cards contain concurrency-relevant evidence (e.g., Java/Go implementation data), both sides' arguments should be re-run against them.

result.position: Default integers carry a no-overflow contract: overflow is a source-level contract violation, mandatorily trap-checked in debug builds, and assumed absent (lowered with nsw/nuw so violation yields poison-style deferred UB) in release builds, giving the optimizer the full LLVM flag-and-fact system. Explicit wrapping/checked/saturating operators exist as opt-in verbose forms. For floating point the corpus supports only an architectural stance — strict semantics by default with relaxations expressed as explicit, scope-local, poison-flag-like contracts rather than a global fast-math mode — with the actual FP default flagged as an open evidence gap.
result.coreArgument:
The language is optimizer-first and LLVM-targeted, and the single best-attested fact in the numeric corpus is that LLVM's optimization power over integer arithmetic is delivered through exactly the semantics I am assigned: overflow-freedom as a contract whose violation is deferred UB (poison), not a defined value and not an immediate trap. N002 confirms that unflagged `add` wraps while `nuw`/`nsw` make overflow poison, and that LLVM's own UB manual frames this as 'overflow facts are contracts, not mere annotations.' A source language whose default integer type states 'overflow is a contract violation' is the only default that lets the frontend emit nsw/nuw on ordinary arithmetic honestly — and honesty is mandatory: the corpus's implementation warning (numeric-semantics-and-poison.md) and the misleading-comparisons note that 'IR metadata solves source design' is misleading both say flags may be emitted only when source semantics make them true. Wrapping-by-default makes nsw emission a lie; checked-by-default makes every add a side-effecting operation; only contract-by-default makes the strongest IR encoding a faithful translation. This is the F009 lesson applied to numerics: an optimizer-first pipeline works best when source semantics are preserved into the IR contract with no semantic gap to bridge.

Why poison-style deferred UB specifically, rather than trap-in-release or immediate UB? The corpus gives a mechanism-level answer. N001 confirms the definedness lattice (immediate UB > poison > undef > freeze(poison) > concrete) and that valid transforms move toward more-defined semantics: poison is the designed-for-speculation point on that lattice. N003 confirms the contrast case — immediate UB (integer division by zero) explicitly blocks hoisting a udiv above its guard. Trapping semantics in release is even more constraining than immediate UB in this respect: a trap is observable behavior that must be preserved and ordered. Poison is the unique choice that lets overflow-carrying computations be speculated, reordered, and folded freely, with UB materializing only at side-effecting uses. Deferred-UB-in-release is therefore not an accident of C history; it is the semantics the LLVM optimizer contract is built around.

The choice also composes. N004 (range metadata: out-of-range loads/returns become poison) and N005 (inbounds GEP: out-of-object results become poison, plus provenance rules) show that LLVM's entire numeric-and-pointer fact channel is poison-based. An optimizer-first language will want to feed the optimizer range facts, index-inbounds facts, and overflow facts; making default-integer overflow a poison-style contract puts all three on the same lattice with the same violation semantics, so frontend obligations, the debug checker, and any future verifier share one story. And N006 shows this is the best-validated corner of the optimizer: InstCombine's nsw/nuw-sensitive folds are expected to carry flag-interaction tests and Alive2 proofs. Adopting the semantics the ecosystem already proof-checks minimizes miscompilation risk relative to inventing a bespoke overflow semantics that would need bespoke passes and bespoke validation.

Mandatory debug checking is what separates my position from C's indefensible version of it. The contract is one semantics with two conforming enforcement modes: debug enforces it dynamically (trap exactly at violation), release refines it (assumes it, per the N001 refinement direction). For every contract-respecting program the two modes agree; for violating programs, debug gives a deterministic, immediate diagnostic that wrapping (silent wrong values, never caught) and C-style release-only UB (never caught) both lack. This mirrors the corpus's aliasing precedent: F001 and F003 show that restrict/noalias-class contracts whose truth 'depends on programmer truthfulness' are already how the highest-value optimizer facts are delivered, and that the frontend-obligation burden is managed, not avoided, in every surveyed language. If the language accepts contract-based non-interference for aliasing — its central optimizer enabler per F001 — accepting contract-based overflow-freedom for integers is the same design move with a strictly better enforcement story, because overflow, unlike aliasing, is cheaply and exactly checkable at runtime in debug.

Finally, the ground rule that soundness may not be sacrificed is a constraint I meet by construction rather than by evasion: the source semantics is well-defined (overflow produces a contract-violation value; observable use of it is erroneous), debug builds enforce it, and N001's freeze gives a principled tool to contain poison at designated boundaries (FFI, persisted state) if the final design demands that no unchecked poison reach observable effects. Whether that containment discipline should be mandatory is a genuine open design question I list under gaps — but the primitive vocabulary (poison + freeze + mandatory checks) is exactly what LLVM verified documentation provides, and no rival default uses it as directly.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Core mechanism: nsw/nuw make signed/unsigned overflow produce poison rather than a defined value, and LLVM's UB manual frames overflow-freedom as a contract central to optimization. This is the exact IR encoding my source default lowers to, so my position is the only default under which the frontend can emit LLVM's strongest integer flags truthfully on unannotated code.

Establishes the definedness lattice (immediate UB > poison > undef > freeze(poison) > concrete) and that valid transforms move toward more-defined semantics. This grounds two claims: (a) poison is the speculation-friendly point on the lattice, so deferred UB preserves more transform freedom than immediate UB or traps; (b) debug-checked / release-assumed is a principled refinement pair, not two divergent languages. Also supplies freeze as a containment primitive and warns off ever exposing undef-like per-use nondeterminism at source level.

The contrast case: division by zero is immediate UB and LLVM explicitly refuses to hoist udiv above its guard. This is direct corpus evidence that trap-like/immediate-UB semantics blocks speculation that poison permits — the mechanism-level argument for deferring UB in release. It also honestly bounds my position: div/rem overflow (INT_MIN/-1, /0) sits outside the poison story and needs a separate design decision.

Range metadata makes out-of-range values poison — showing LLVM's numeric fact channel is uniformly poison-based. A poison-style overflow default composes with range facts on one lattice with one violation semantics, so the same contract machinery (debug checks, frontend obligations) serves both.

Inbounds GEP out-of-object results are poison, with provenance rules optimizers rely on. Index arithmetic and overflow contracts interact (trip counts, address computation); this card shows the pointer-side contract is also poison-style, reinforcing that a single deferred-UB contract system spans integers and indexing.

InstCombine expects nsw/nuw- and fast-math-flag-sensitive transforms to carry flag-interaction tests and Alive2 proofs. Supports (a) miscompilation-risk argument: my semantics rides the most proof-validated rewrite corpus in LLVM; (b) my FP architectural stance: fast-math flags are treated as poison-generating-flag-like contracts needing the same care, favoring scope-local explicit FP relaxation contracts over a global mode. Note this card is medium source-accuracy (one 2-1 vote).

Precedent: the corpus's central optimizer enablers are language-level non-interference contracts whose truth in restrict-like forms 'depends on programmer truthfulness or unsafe assumptions.' If the language accepts contract-based aliasing facts as its core value proposition, contract-based overflow facts are the same design move — and overflow contracts are cheaply runtime-checkable in debug, which aliasing contracts generally are not.

Violating no-alias IR facts is UB owned by the IR contract, and metadata is sound only if frontend obligations are true. Supports my framing that the language must make overflow-freedom a real source semantic (contract + mandatory debug enforcement) precisely so nsw emission satisfies the frontend-obligation requirement, rather than emitting flags on a wrapping or unspecified semantics.

Optimization-first design benefits from IRs that preserve source semantics long enough to optimize them. Choosing source overflow semantics isomorphic to the LLVM flag contract means zero semantic gap at lowering: the source contract IS the IR contract, nothing is lost or fabricated in translation.

concessions: Every N card carries design_generalization_confidence: low, and the corpus's own synthesis (numeric-semantics-and-poison.md 'Still undecided') explicitly lists checked-vs-wrapping-vs-trapping-vs-poison defaults and source-level-UB acceptability as open questions. My argument shows my position aligns best with the LLVM contract layer; it cannot claim corpus proof that this is the best source-language policy.

The corpus contains zero quantified performance evidence. The claimed optimization wins of nsw over wrapping or checked arithmetic (trip-count analysis, induction-variable widening, vectorization) are mechanism-plausible via N001/N002/N003 but unbenchmarked here; caveats.md warns documentation evidence 'does not quantify performance magnitude.' If measured deltas turn out small on representative workloads, the safety cost of my position buys little.

Mandatory debug checking does not make the release-mode promise true; it makes violations likelier to be caught before release. Release soundness remains conditional on test coverage. A program with an overflow reachable only on untested inputs is well-defined and diagnosed in debug but has genuine UB in release — this is the honest core weakness, and it is exactly the 'programmer truthfulness' caveat F001 attaches to restrict-class contracts.

If the debate's 'soundness may not be sacrificed' rule is read strictly as 'no UB reachable from non-unsafe source code,' my position as stated fails that bar and would need amendment (e.g., mandatory freeze/check at observable boundaries per N001, overflow contracts admitted only inside a delimited contract/unsafe system, or a verifier discharging no-overflow obligations SPARK-style per F006's general pattern). I argue the contract reading is the right one for an optimizer-first language, but that is a philosophical choice the evidence does not settle.

My position statement covers default integers only. N003 shows div/rem sits in the immediate-UB regime in LLVM (divide-by-zero, and sdiv INT_MIN/-1), so division needs its own policy (guards, traps, or preconditions) — 'poison-style default' is not a complete integer story.

On floating point I can defend only an architecture (strict default, explicit scope-local relaxation contracts, N006's flag-care as weak support), not a decision: the corpus explicitly defers strict-vs-fast defaults, FMA/contraction, reassociation, rounding, NaN/signed-zero, and reproducibility.

The N-card corpus itself warns that its workflow had many stalled subagents — coverage is strong for surviving LLVM findings but incomplete overall, so absence of corpus evidence against my position is weak evidence of anything.
risks: Release-mode exploit surface: deferred UB on overflow in C/C++ has a well-known history of security-relevant miscompiles (bounds checks folded away, etc.). This history is NOT carded in my assigned corpus — I flag it as an uncarded but decision-relevant risk rather than pretend it away. Debug checking reduces but cannot eliminate it.

Debug/release divergence: bugs that manifest only in release (where the violated contract was assumed) are the hardest class to diagnose, and the divergence is by design in my position. Mitigations (release-with-checks build flavor, freeze-at-boundary discipline) cost either performance or design complexity.

Erosion of the safety net: if mandatory debug checks carry heavy runtime cost, developers may test predominantly in release, converting 'caught in debug' from a guarantee into an aspiration. No corpus evidence exists on debug-check efficacy or cost.

Frontend correctness landmines: poison semantics is subtle (propagation rules, branch-on-poison, flag-preservation under rewrites). N001's note that per-use undef nondeterminism invalidated rewrites like x*2 -> x+x, and N006's requirement of Alive2 proofs and negative tests for flag-sensitive folds, both indicate this is an area where informal reasoning miscompiles; the language's own mid-level IR and lowering would inherit that proof burden.

Default-choice lock-in: defaults are nearly impossible to change post-1.0. If later benchmarking shows checked-in-release costs are acceptable (as Swift chose), a poison default would have been the wrong irreversible bet; conversely the reverse migration (checked -> poison) is semantically easier. This asymmetry argues mildly against my position and I state it rather than hide it.

The corpus is selection-biased (caveats.md, misleading-comparisons entry 1): its contract-centric worldview comes from aliasing-heavy sampling, and my argument leans on analogy to that worldview (F001/F003). If numerics-focused evidence gathering had run to completion, the balance of cards might look different.
evidenceGaps: Quantified benchmarks: measured performance deltas of nsw-style vs wrapping (-fwrapv) vs checked (-ftrapv / Swift / Rust debug) integer semantics on representative optimizer-sensitive workloads (loop-heavy, vectorizable, index-arithmetic-heavy). Nothing in the corpus quantifies this; my position's entire payoff claim rests on it.

Empirical cost of checked-in-release: modern branch-predictor-era overhead of overflow checks (Swift production data, Rust overflow-checks=on studies). If this is consistently low single-digit percent, my position's advantage over trap-in-release shrinks to the speculation/vectorization cases and needs re-argument.

Debug-check efficacy data: how often mandatory debug/test-time overflow checking actually catches violations before release (e.g., Rust ecosystem experience with debug-mode panics), which determines how much the 'caught in debug' half of my position is worth.
Security incidence: carded evidence on exploit classes attributable to deferred-UB overflow in C/C++ versus checked languages, to price the release-UB risk honestly.

Formal source-level soundness: literature on giving poison-style deferred UB a rigorous source semantics (e.g., the LLVM 'taming UB' line of work, Vellvm/Alive2 formalizations) to establish whether 'contract violation as poison + freeze at boundaries' can satisfy a strict no-unsound-semantics rule. Not in corpus.

Floating-point evidence across the board: strict IEEE vs fast-math defaults, FMA/contraction, reassociation, reproducibility, GPU math — explicitly listed as undecided in numeric-semantics-and-poison.md; my FP stance is provisional architecture only.

Division/remainder policy: evidence on the cost of guarding vs trapping vs precondition-contracts for div-by-zero and INT_MIN/-1, since N003 shows these are immediate UB in LLVM and outside the poison mechanism.

Missing card families: the debate brief references A001-A010, D001-D010, C001-C006, J001-J005, but none of the five files I was directed to read contain them; any support or refutation those provisional direct-extraction cards hold for overflow semantics is unexamined in this argument and must be reconciled before a decision.

result.position: Message-passing core: shared mutable memory does not exist in the core language. Cross-thread communication is exclusively via channels/actors that transfer ownership of data (linear/affine hand-off, use-after-send is a type error). Atomics and shared-memory primitives exist only in an explicit, visibly-marked systems module whose orderings map one-to-one onto LLVM's graded atomic orderings.
result.coreArgument: For an optimizer-first language, the pivotal evidence is C003: every classic optimization freedom the corpus lists — introducing writes on paths, re-loading a value, caching values in registers across iterations, moving code past fences — is lost precisely when memory *may* be shared. The question is therefore not 'how do we handle races' but 'how much memory can the compiler prove is unshared, and how cheaply.' A message-passing core gives the maximal, structurally-guaranteed answer: if shared mutable memory is unrepresentable in the core, then every non-atomic access in core code is unshared by construction — no programmer-supplied contracts, no per-type Send/Sync analysis, no escape hatch that silently voids the invariant. This matters for soundness of the lowering path specifically: F003 warns that LLVM noalias/alias.scope metadata is sound only if frontend obligations are actually true, and C005 notes LLVM treats racing non-atomic loads as undef — a whole-language no-cross-thread-sharing invariant is the easiest such obligation to discharge soundly, because it is a closed-world structural fact rather than a contract (contrast F001's caveat that restrict-like contracts depend on programmer truthfulness). The corpus's best-attested optimizer enablers are non-interference and ownership transfer (F001, F002, F006); ownership-transferring channels are the strongest form of both: cross-thread interference around writes (F002's core mechanism) is impossible, and the transfer discipline is directly precedented by SPARK's ownership-with-transfer policy (F006) and Rust's &mut exclusivity (F001). The position also dissolves, for core code, the unresolved UB-vs-bounded race dilemma (C002): races are unrepresentable, so we pay neither UB blast radius nor bounded-race optimization constraints, and DRF-SC (C001) holds trivially with channel send/receive as the only happens-before edges — a single, simple publication mechanism that generalizes the narrow publication carve-out Java needed final fields for (C006). Crucially, this is not capability loss: the systems module deliberately exposes the full LLVM ordering gradation (C005), so experts write lock-free code with exactly the pass-freedom tradeoffs LLVM documents — but the contamination boundary is explicit, module-scoped, and optimizer-visible, so the pessimization C003 describes is confined to code that opted into it instead of leaking into every function that 'might' see shared memory. Under this debate's rule that ergonomics may be sacrificed but soundness may not, this is the only candidate position where race-freedom of core code does not rest on unverified programmer assertions or unsafe-code discipline (C004's own negative scope: unsafe code and incorrect Send/Sync impls are UB).

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Enumerates the concrete optimizer freedoms lost whenever memory may be shared (no introduced writes, no re-loading, no register caching across iterations, fences as motion barriers). This is the direct payoff case: a core in which sharing is unrepresentable restores all of these freedoms on all non-atomic core memory, globally, rather than only where analysis or annotations prove non-sharing.

Establishes that the optimization value of alias information is specifically proving non-interference around writes. Message passing with ownership transfer makes cross-thread write interference impossible by construction — the maximal instance of the mechanism this card identifies as the value driver.

Language-level non-interference rules (Rust &mut exclusivity, Fortran dummy arguments, SPARK anti-aliasing) are the corpus's central attested optimizer enablers; its caveat that restrict-like contracts depend on programmer truthfulness cuts in my favor — a structural no-sharing core needs no such trust.

SPARK gives pointer-like access types an ownership policy with transfer semantics for verification-oriented, optimizer-friendly semantics — the closest corpus precedent for 'ownership moves via transfer' as a sound language-level discipline, which channels generalize to the cross-thread case.

LLVM noalias/alias.scope metadata is sound only if frontend obligations are true and survive lowering. A whole-language invariant that core memory is never cross-thread shared is the cheapest and most robust way to discharge those obligations, versus per-annotation or per-type proofs that unsafe code can silently invalidate.

Documents that the field is split on racy-program semantics (UB vs bounded) with no performance quantification either way. My position confines this unresolved dilemma to the explicit systems module: core code has no racy executions to give semantics to, so the language need not bet on either side for the bulk of code.

DRF-SC is the convergent baseline contract across major languages. A message-passing core satisfies it trivially — core programs are data-race-free by construction — and channel send/receive provides the happens-before edges the contract requires, as a single publication mechanism.

LLVM's graded orderings (NotAtomic through SequentiallyConsistent, each enabling/forbidding specific transformations) supply the exact vocabulary for my systems module, so quarantining atomics loses no expert capability; and its note that racing non-atomic loads are undef in LLVM IR shows why the frontend's 'non-atomic implies unshared' assumption should be structural rather than trusted.

Cited honestly as the strongest counter-precedent I must answer: Rust achieves 'optimizer may treat non-atomic memory as unshared unless types say otherwise' via Send/Sync plus borrowing, without banning shared memory. I use it two ways: it proves sharing-as-a-type-level-fact is viable (my channels rely on the same kind of type-level ownership fact), and its negative scope (unsafe code and incorrect Send/Sync impls are UB) is precisely the trust surface my structural approach removes from the core.

Java final fields show languages need a publication-safety carve-out (readers see initialized state without synchronization) and how narrow and precondition-laden it is when bolted onto shared memory. Channel transfer subsumes publication safety: the receive edge is the publication, with no escape-during-construction hazard.

Used against myself as a concession anchor: escape analysis in managed runtimes recovers allocation/synchronization costs only conditionally (inlining-, profile-, implementation-dependent). If channel/actor mailboxes force heap allocation and copying, recovery of that cost via analysis is exactly the kind of conditional, non-guaranteed optimization an optimizer-first language should be reluctant to depend on.

concessions: The corpus contains zero direct evidence on message-passing or actor systems: no cards on Erlang, Pony, Go channels, Swift actors, or any channel/actor memory model or implementation. The implementation file explicitly lists 'Swift/Ada/Pony/Erlang models' and 'empirical cost data' as open items, and caveats.md says concurrency coverage is thin and the corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection. My position is therefore an extrapolation from non-interference/ownership evidence (F001/F002/F006), not directly attested.

C004 is a genuine rival: Rust demonstrably achieves the same optimizer-relevant per-location fact (non-atomic memory treatable as unshared unless types say otherwise) without banning shared mutable memory. The marginal optimizer benefit of my stronger structural ban over a Rust-style Send/Sync + borrowing design is unquantified by any card — my advantage claim rests on the soundness/trust-surface argument, not on demonstrated extra optimization.

Channels do not eliminate concurrency nondeterminism: message-ordering races, deadlocks, and protocol errors remain fully possible. My position removes memory races (the C003-relevant kind), not races in general; no card addresses whether that residual nondeterminism is acceptable.

The systems module is load-bearing, not optional: channels themselves must be implemented with atomics/locks from that module, and much high-performance code (in-place parallel array updates, work-stealing, shared read-mostly structures) may live there. If most performance-critical code opts out of the core, the global optimizer win shrinks toward what a per-type approach already gives — no card quantifies how much code stays in the clean core.

Ownership transfer via channels requires linear/affine typing to prevent use-after-send, so my position does not avoid building Rust-grade ownership machinery; it relocates it. Per the misleading-comparisons note, I also must not overclaim non-interference as 'the strongest optimizer enabler' — the corpus is selection-biased toward it.
Documentation-grade evidence (all F and C cards) shows what languages/compilers claim and enable, but per caveats.md does not quantify performance magnitude for any of these mechanisms.
risks: FFI/foreign-thread unsoundness: the implementation file's open question — what can foreign threads, callbacks, signal handlers, and unsafe code mutate — is existential for my position. If foreign code can alias core memory, the structural 'unshared' invariant that justifies emitting aggressive noalias facts (F003) is broken, and per C005 racing non-atomic accesses become undef at the IR level. The FFI boundary must copy, pin, or type-quarantine memory, and no corpus evidence establishes a sound, cheap design for that.

Mandatory data movement cost: ownership transfer avoids logical copying, but channel implementations impose queueing, synchronization, and possibly allocation per message. F007 warns that recovering such costs via analysis (escape-analysis-style) is conditional, not guaranteed. For fine-grained parallelism (e.g., parallel loops over one array), per-message overhead could dominate, forcing everything into the systems module — the two-language problem inside one language.

Ecosystem bifurcation: if the systems module is where real performance work happens, the optimizer-friendly core becomes a coordination layer and the language's headline claim ('optimizer-first') is carried by the quarantined shared-memory dialect, where C003's constraints apply in full anyway.

Expressiveness cliff for shared read-only data: broadcast-immutable sharing (read-mostly caches, large lookup tables) is safe and optimizer-friendly (F001/F006 note benign/read-only aliasing is permitted even in SPARK/Fortran), but a naive 'no shared memory' core forbids it, forcing copies or systems-module escapes. The core likely needs an immutable-sharing carve-out, which reintroduces design complexity (freeze semantics, publication — cf. C006's preconditions).

Overclaiming risk: per the misleading-comparisons file, IR metadata does not solve source design and non-interference's prominence in the corpus is selection bias; a decision made on my argument alone would be resting on a corpus that has not adversarially examined message passing at all.
evidenceGaps: No evidence cards on any message-passing/actor language memory model or implementation (Erlang, Pony, Go channels, Swift actors) — explicitly listed as open in /Users/bytedance/Dev/xlang/optimizer-language-research/implementation/concurrency-atomics-memory-model.md. This must be closed before my position can win.

No empirical cost data comparing ownership-transferring channels versus shared-memory synchronization on performance-critical workloads (message overhead, allocation pressure, throughput/latency of fine-grained parallelism).

No evidence on whether a whole-program structural no-sharing invariant lowers to measurably more aggressive LLVM optimization than per-type Send/Sync facts (the marginal benefit over the C004/Rust route) — needs compiler-level experiments emitting noalias/alias.scope under both regimes.
No evidence on sound FFI designs for a no-shared-memory core: how foreign threads, callbacks, and signal handlers are prevented from aliasing core memory, and at what cost.

No evidence on what fraction of realistic high-performance code can remain in a message-passing core versus escaping to the systems module (determines how much of the C003 payoff is actually realized).

No evidence on semantics/cost of an immutable-sharing carve-out (freeze/publication rules) that a practical message-passing core would likely need; C006 is the only adjacent card and is FAQ-grade with strong preconditions.
Primary sources still unfetched per the implementation file: C++ standard memory-model text and JLS chapter 17; C001/C002 rest partly on a secondary survey.

The debate ground rules reference card series N001-N006, A001-A010, D001-D010, and J001-J005, but none of these appear in the five files I was instructed to read; I could not weigh or cite them, and any of them bearing on concurrency or actor systems could change this case.

result.position: MLIR-first: define a custom high-level MLIR dialect that carries ownership/aliasing, effects, shape/layout, iteration-structure, and schedule facts as first-class IR structure; reuse affine/linalg/vector/memref infrastructure where the facts fit; progressively lower to LLVM, emitting LLVM noalias attributes/metadata only as the final, derived encoding of facts already verified at the dialect level (the Mojo/Flang direction).
result.coreArgument:
The debate question is which strategy best PRESERVES source-level facts for the optimizer, and the corpus's strongest confirmed finding on IR architecture points directly at the high-level-IR-before-LLVM pattern: F009 (workflow-confirmed, 3-0) shows Swift deliberately optimizes on SIL, an SSA IR that keeps Swift-specific semantics (ownership, explicit ARC operations) visible to passes BEFORE LLVM IR generation, precisely because LLVM would not otherwise see that information. MLIR-first is the generalization of that confirmed lesson: get the SIL-style architectural benefit without building a bespoke IR stack from scratch, by hosting our facts in a custom dialect on shared MLIR infrastructure.

The case has three legs. (1) The facts worth preserving are established: F001 and F002 (confirmed) establish that language-level non-interference around writes is the central optimizer enabler (register promotion, load/store elimination, reordering), and F006 shows ownership/effect restrictions are a viable source of those facts. An optimizer-first language will therefore generate exactly the kind of high-value facts that must survive to the passes that consume them. (2) The direct-to-LLVM alternative carries these facts in a medium that is discardable and UB-dangerous: F003 (confirmed) says LLVM noalias/alias.scope metadata expresses the facts but is 'only sound if frontend obligations are true and metadata survives lowering,' and violating it is UB; our own pass-consumer matrix (llvm-pass-consumer-matrix.csv) lists 'lowering strips metadata' and 'false noalias causes miscompile' as the live hazards for ScopedNoAliasAA and 'metadata dropped' for LoopVectorize. A first-class dialect op or type is structurally part of the IR — it cannot be silently dropped by a metadata-agnostic pass; it must be explicitly consumed by a lowering that we control and can verify, and dialect verifiers give a place to enforce the frontend obligations F003 warns about before any UB-bearing LLVM encoding is emitted. Late, derived emission of noalias (including the very strong return-value contract of F004) from verified dialect structure is the soundest available route to those LLVM facts. (3) The reuse leg: A001-A004 (provisional direct-extraction) document that MLIR memref already carries shape/strides/memory-space and explicit no-alias/UB-bearing contracts (distinct_objects) in optimizer-visible IR; linalg preserves iteration spaces, indexing maps, and parallel/reduction iterator kinds for tiling/fusion/vectorization; affine makes dependence analysis tractable by restricting index forms; and vector provides staged n-D vector semantics with progressive lowering above scalar loads/stores. These are precisely the fact categories in our fit/gap table, already represented and consumed by existing transformations. A005 closes the loop downstream: LLVM's vectorizers need alias/dependence/reduction/FP-flag legality facts, so either we vectorize at the linalg/vector level where those facts are structural, or we emit LLVM with the facts intact — MLIR-first supports both; direct-to-LLVM supports only the fragile second path.

Because the language is optimizer-first and may sacrifice ergonomics, the classic objection to affine/linalg — 'real programs do not fit the restricted forms' (A003 negative scope) — is weaker here than anywhere else: we can REQUIRE hot code to be expressible in affine/structured form, the same move Futhark makes with regular arrays and uniqueness (A008), ISPC with uniform/varying (A007), Halide with an explicit schedule layer separated from the pure algorithm (A006), and Fortran DO CONCURRENT with independence restrictions (A010). A schedule-carrying dialect is the natural MLIR-hosted home for the A006 lesson, and 'schedule facts' have no plausible carrier at the LLVM IR level at all. Finally, versus the bespoke-IR alternative (build our own SIL): F009 confirms the architecture works but its negative scope stresses it is implementation-specific and expensive to keep coherent (OSSA/non-OSSA phases, stale docs); MLIR gives the same fact-preservation architecture while amortizing the pass manager, verifier framework, and the affine/linalg/vector transformations we would otherwise rebuild — though I flag the engineering-cost comparison itself as an evidence gap, not a carded fact.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Strongest confirmed card for the position's core architecture: Swift SIL is an SSA IR that preserves source-level semantics (ownership, explicit ARC ops) so optimization happens before LLVM IR generation, 'so passes can use information LLVM would not otherwise see.' MLIR-first is this exact pattern with reusable infrastructure instead of a bespoke IR.

Confirms LLVM can encode no-alias facts but its negative scope — metadata is only sound if frontend obligations are true and it survives lowering, with UB on violation — is the central weakness of the direct-to-LLVM alternative. A dialect with verifiers is where those obligations can be checked before the UB-bearing encoding is emitted.

The strong allocation-like noalias return contract requires 'lifetime/provenance/escape rules' at the source level before it can be emitted. A high-level dialect carrying ownership/escape facts is the natural place to prove that obligation and then emit F004's contract late and soundly.

Establishes that language-level non-interference rules (Rust exclusivity, Fortran dummy args, SPARK ownership) are the central optimizer enablers — i.e., the facts our pipeline must preserve are exactly the ownership/aliasing facts the proposed dialect is designed to carry.

Identifies what the preserved facts buy: register promotion, redundant-read/dead-write elimination, reordering — all gated on proving non-interference around writes. This defines the payload the IR strategy must deliver intact to consumers.

SPARK's ownership-with-transfer and side-effect-free expression rules show effects/ownership facts can be made checkable rather than trusted — supporting a dialect whose verifier enforces obligations instead of trusting frontend-emitted metadata.

(Provisional) MemRef already carries shape, strides, offsets, memory spaces, view transforms, and explicit no-alias/UB-bearing contracts (distinct_objects) in optimizer-visible IR — direct evidence the MLIR substrate represents our fact categories today rather than requiring everything from scratch.

(Provisional) Linalg preserves iteration spaces, indexing maps, and parallel/reduction iterator kinds specifically to support tiling, fusion, promotion, vectorization — structured-loop facts that have no first-class LLVM IR carrier.

(Provisional) Affine shows restricting index forms makes dependence analysis tractable; an optimizer-first language that can mandate affine-expressible hot loops maps onto existing dependence/transform infrastructure instead of reproving it post-lowering.

(Provisional) The vector dialect's staged n-D vectors with progressive lowering preserve vector/memory-movement semantics above scalar load/store form until target lowering — the progressive-lowering mechanism my position depends on, demonstrated for one fact family.

(Provisional) LLVM vectorizers consume alias/dependence, reduction/induction, FP-flag, and loop-shape facts; this names the downstream consumers that direct-to-LLVM must feed via droppable metadata, whereas MLIR-first can either vectorize where facts are structural or emit that metadata late from verified structure.

(Provisional) Halide's explicit schedule layer shows loop order/tiling/vectorization choices can be program structure separate from algorithm semantics — the 'schedule facts' in my assigned position need an IR home, and a custom MLIR dialect is the only candidate among the debated strategies with existing analogous infrastructure.

(Provisional) Futhark's regular arrays, size types, and uniqueness/consumption show a language can trade expressiveness for optimization-friendly facts — supporting the claim that an optimizer-first language can legislate its programs into the forms affine/linalg represent well, neutralizing the main fit objection.

(Provisional) ISPC's uniform/varying and mask semantics are language-level lane facts that would need a carrier above scalar LLVM IR; supports the breadth of fact categories a high-level dialect must and can host.

(Provisional) DO CONCURRENT encodes independence intent with restrictions that are 'necessary rather than sufficient' — evidence that independence facts need to be carried alongside verification obligations, which is a dialect-verifier shape of problem, not a metadata shape.

Fortran's optimization-friendly aliasing model is the semantic basis Flang lowers through MLIR (FIR) — the direction named in my position; supports that the fact families involved (dummy-argument non-interference, TARGET-narrowed pointer aliasing) are real and language-enforceable, though the corpus does not verify Flang's MLIR pipeline itself.

concessions: The corpus explicitly lacks adversarially verified coverage of MLIR and Mojo (caveats.md: no verified coverage for 'Mojo, MLIR' among others). Every MLIR-specific card I cite (A001-A005) is direct-extraction and provisional; my only workflow-confirmed architectural evidence (F009) is about Swift SIL, an analogy, and its negative scope warns it is implementation-specific.

MLIR does not automatically solve fact preservation: progressive lowering can drop facts at every dialect boundary just as LLVM passes drop metadata. The misleading-comparisons file's warning that 'IR mechanisms are conditional carriers of source facts' applies to my position too — the advantage is that dialect structure forces explicit, testable lowering decisions, not that preservation is free.

Our own fit/gap analysis (implementation/mlir-dialect-fit-gap.md) is almost entirely TBD: for alias/noalias, array layout/shape, and effects, the columns 'where it could live', 'passes preserving it', and 'where it may be dropped' are unfilled, and two rows say 'missing primary sources'. The reuse claim is currently documented intent (A-cards), not a completed fit assessment.

If the final pipeline still relies on LLVM's LoopVectorize/SLP/ScopedNoAliasAA after lowering, the metadata-fragility and false-noalias-miscompile hazards in the pass-consumer matrix apply to MLIR-first as well; the strategy narrows the fragile window, it does not eliminate it.

No card quantifies performance, compile-time, or engineering cost for any strategy (caveats.md: documentation evidence 'does not quantify performance magnitude, code-size impact, compile-time cost'). My claim that MLIR reuse is cheaper than a bespoke SIL-style IR is an engineering judgment, not carded evidence.

Affine/linalg/vector fit structured array/loop code; the corpus offers no evidence they help general-purpose code with pointers, irregular control flow, or rich effects — for that code the custom dialect would carry ownership/effects facts with no existing MLIR transformations to reuse, weakening the reuse leg to just infrastructure (pass manager, verifiers, lowering framework).
risks: Unverified-substrate risk: betting the compiler architecture on MLIR components whose documented behavior (A001-A004) has not been adversarially verified; if memref/linalg semantics or the distinct_objects contract differ in practice from docs, the fit/gap picture changes materially.

Two-layer soundness risk: facts must now survive dialect-to-dialect lowering AND dialect-to-LLVM lowering; each boundary is a place where an ownership/effects fact can be dropped (performance loss) or mistranslated into false LLVM noalias (miscompile, per F003/matrix hazards). More layers means more preservation obligations to test.

Novel-dialect risk: ownership and effects dialects do not exist upstream; we would design UB-bearing contracts ourselves. F008's history is a warning — two overbroad LLVM EH claims were refuted in this corpus, showing how easily IR-contract folklore goes wrong; our own dialect contracts would have no community review initially.

Ecosystem drift and version risk: caveats.md flags LLVM/MLIR docs as version-sensitive (LLVM 23.0.0git); upstream dialect churn (linalg/vector evolve rapidly) could invalidate the reuse assumption or impose continuous migration cost.

Opportunity-cost risk: if the language's hot code turns out not to be array/loop-shaped, the affine/linalg/vector reuse — the main differentiator versus a bespoke IR — delivers little, and MLIR-first degenerates into 'bespoke IR with extra framework dependency'.

Overreach risk baked into my own framing: the corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection (caveats.md), so an MLIR dialect designed today would optimize for carrying aliasing facts and might under-provision for numerics, concurrency, layout, or dispatch facts where we have no confirmed evidence at all.
evidenceGaps: Card sets N001-N006, D001-D010, C001-C006, and J001-J005 were referenced in the debate brief but are not present in any of the seven files I was directed to read; I could not weigh or cite them. This must be reconciled before judging the debate.

Complete implementation/mlir-dialect-fit-gap.md with primary MLIR sources: for each fact (alias/noalias, layout/shape, effects, ownership, schedule), which dialect construct carries it, which passes preserve it, where it is dropped, and what the verifier must check. Currently all TBD.

Adversarial verification of A001-A005 (MLIR memref/linalg/affine/vector and LLVM vectorizer cards) to promote them from provisional to confirmed; caveats.md explicitly excludes MLIR from verified coverage.

Empirical fact-preservation evidence from existing MLIR-first frontends (Flang/FIR, Mojo, IREE): does an ownership/aliasing/shape fact demonstrably survive to a consuming pass more reliably than the equivalent LLVM-metadata route? No card addresses this; it is the decisive comparative question.

A working negative-and-positive test harness of the kind the pass-consumer matrix already prescribes (FileCheck metadata tests, allocation-elision tests, vectorization-remark tests) run over both candidate pipelines, to measure where facts are actually dropped.
Quantified compile-time and engineering cost of an MLIR pipeline versus direct-to-LLVM and versus a bespoke SIL-style IR; no card quantifies any cost dimension.

Evidence that ownership/effects contracts of the F001/F006 kind can be encoded as MLIR dialect ops/types with sound verifiers — F009 shows a custom IR (SIL) can host ownership, but no source shows MLIR specifically hosting a verified ownership discipline.

Coverage of fact families beyond aliasing (numerics, concurrency, dispatch, data layout) — the corpus is selection-biased toward aliasing (misleading-comparisons entry 1), so any dialect design would currently be evidence-blind in those areas.

result.position: Direct-to-LLVM with maximal metadata: skip a bespoke MLIR pipeline; have the frontend emit LLVM IR immediately with the richest sound fact encoding LLVM natively defines — noalias parameter/return attributes, alias.scope/noalias scoped metadata, nsw/range/fast-math flags, and TBAA-style metadata — in the Rust/Clang direction, with language semantics designed so those frontend obligations are true by construction.
result.coreArgument:
The question is what best preserves source-level facts FOR THE OPTIMIZER, and on the evidence we actually have, the optimizer is LLVM's pass pipeline and its documented input vocabulary — not a hypothetical dialect stack. Three lines of verified evidence converge on direct emission.

(1) The destination format for our best-attested facts already exists and is verified. The single strongest evidence cluster in this corpus is non-interference: language-level exclusivity and no-alias rules are central optimizer enablers (F001), and their payoff — register promotion, redundant-read elimination, dead-store elimination, reordering — is delivered by compiler passes consuming alias/mod-ref facts (F002). LLVM IR has a documented, workflow-verified encoding for exactly these facts: noalias attributes and alias.scope/noalias scoped metadata expressing disjoint access sets, with ScopedNoAliasAA's subset/superset consumption rule corroborated in implementation source (F003). LLVM's native vocabulary is not a lossy least-common-denominator here — its noalias return attribute is strictly STRONGER than anything C99 restrict can say at source level, modeling allocation-like freshness disjoint from all caller-accessible storage (F004). Direct emission lets an optimizer-first language mint that maximal-strength contract the moment the frontend can prove it, with zero intermediate translations in which it could be weakened or dropped.

(2) The concrete consumers are LLVM passes, and LLVM-level facts are documented as sufficient inputs. The corpus's own pass-consumer matrix (/Users/bytedance/Dev/xlang/optimizer-language-research/implementation/llvm-pass-consumer-matrix.csv) maps every candidate fact to an LLVM consumer: ScopedNoAliasAA wants alias.scope/noalias metadata (F003); SROA/mem2reg wants escape/lifetime facts; LICM/GVN/DSE want mod/ref and effect facts (F002); LoopVectorize wants no-loop-carried-deps, alignment, and FP flags. Independently, LLVM's vectorizer documentation confirms that legality/profitability turns on alias/dependence facts, reductions/inductions, trip counts, and FP math flags (A005) — precisely the attribute-and-metadata payload my position emits directly. Feeding the consumer its documented diet is the shortest sound path to the benefit.

(3) Every additional IR layer is a place where facts die, and the corpus contains ZERO verified evidence that MLIR preserves ours. The matrix's own hazard column names "lowering strips metadata" as the primary failure mode, and F003's negative scope conditions soundness on metadata surviving lowering. An MLIR pipeline doubles the lowerings (source→dialects→LLVM IR) and therefore doubles the preservation obligations — obligations we would have to design, implement, and adversarially verify ourselves, because the corpus's MLIR fit/gap document (/Users/bytedance/Dev/xlang/optimizer-language-research/implementation/mlir-dialect-fit-gap.md) is entirely TBD ("F003 plus MLIR docs" needed; "missing primary sources" for layout and effects), the MLIR A-cards (A001-A004) are provisional direct extractions, and caveats.md states outright that the corpus lacks verified MLIR coverage. Choosing MLIR today would be choosing the unevidenced branch.

The standard objection is F009 — Swift built SIL because LLVM could not see ARC semantics, so optimize-before-you-lose-it favors a mid-level IR. But F009's actual design lesson is that performance-critical semantics must be VISIBLE to optimizer passes, not that a new IR is the only way to make them visible. Swift retrofitted SIL because Swift's semantics (implicit retain/release) predated and mismatched LLVM's vocabulary. We are designing the language AFTER LLVM, optimizer-first, with license to be ugly: we can co-design source semantics so the facts the language guarantees (F001-style exclusivity, F005-style argument non-interference, F006-style side-effect-free expressions) map onto encodings LLVM already consumes (F003, F004, A005). Where Swift had a representation gap to bridge, we can simply refuse to create one. And soundness — which this debate may not sacrifice — is exactly where direct emission is strongest: the frontend obligations behind noalias are made true by construction in the language semantics (F001), verified by the FileCheck-plus-negative-alias test regime the matrix already prescribes, rather than being re-proved across a stack of custom dialect conversions nobody has verified.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Core load-bearing card: LLVM IR already provides verified, documented encodings for no-alias facts (noalias attributes; alias.scope/noalias scoped metadata with the subset/superset consumption rule corroborated in ScopedNoAliasAA source). The destination format for our best-attested fact class exists today; direct emission targets a verified mechanism instead of an unverified dialect stack.

Shows LLVM's native contract vocabulary can be STRONGER than source-level alternatives: noalias return models allocation-like freshness where C99 restrict is meaningless for returns, and the wording is stable across doc versions. Direct-to-LLVM lets the frontend mint maximal-strength contracts with no intermediate layer that could weaken them.

Identifies the payoff optimizations (register promotion, read/write elimination, reordering, LICM, mem-to-reg promotion) as being performed by LLVM/GCC-style passes consuming alias and mod-ref facts — i.e., the value of preserved facts is realized inside LLVM's pipeline, so delivering facts directly to that pipeline is the shortest sound path.

Establishes that language-level non-interference rules (Rust &mut exclusivity, Fortran dummy-argument rules, SPARK ownership) are the well-attested optimizer enablers. An optimizer-first language can make F003's frontend-soundness precondition true by construction, which is exactly what makes aggressive direct emission of noalias-class metadata safe — the Rust direction my position names.

(Provisional A-card.) LLVM's Loop/SLP vectorizers document consuming alias/dependence facts, reductions/inductions, trip counts, FP math flags, and runtime pointer checks — concrete evidence that LLVM-level attributes and flags are the documented sufficient inputs for a flagship optimization, without a structured mid-level IR.

Fortran shows a source language whose argument non-interference rules compilers may directly assume for optimization (Flang lowers to LLVM); supporting precedent that source-semantics-to-LLVM-assumption is a workable pipeline shape without a language-specific optimizing IR for the aliasing fact class.

SPARK's side-effect-free expressions and ownership policy show a source-semantics route to the effect/purity facts the LICM/GVN/DSE matrix row needs — evidence the language layer, not an intermediate IR, can carry the proof burden my position assigns it.

Cited honestly as the strongest COUNTER-card, which my argument must and does answer: SIL exists because Swift's ARC semantics had no LLVM encoding and Swift-level passes needed pre-LLVM visibility. My answer — co-design the language so its facts are LLVM-encodable — is a design bet whose limit F009 marks; if we acquire semantics with no LLVM encoding, F009's lesson bites.

(Concession side, provisional.) MemRef carries shape/stride/offset/memory-space facts in optimizer-visible IR; evidence that some array-layout facts have MLIR homes with no obvious LLVM attribute equivalent — a real cost of skipping MLIR that I concede rather than dispute.

(Concession side, provisional.) Linalg preserves iteration-space/indexing-map/iterator-kind structure intended for tiling, fusion, and vectorization — transformation-requiring facts that flat LLVM IR plus metadata serves poorly; concession bounding my position to annotation-shaped facts.

(Concession side, provisional.) Affine's restricted index forms make dependence analysis tractable in a way LLVM's general IR does not replicate; supports the risk that loop-restructuring optimizations are weaker on my path.

(Concession side, provisional.) Halide's explicit schedule layer shows optimization choices surfaced as manipulable program structure — a capability class direct-to-LLVM emission cannot express at all; I concede this workload class as out of scope for my strategy or needing source-level scheduling instead.

concessions: F009 is genuine counterevidence: Swift's SIL demonstrates real value from optimizing language-specific semantics before LLVM IR generation, and if our language ends up with performance-critical semantics lacking any LLVM attribute/metadata encoding (ownership transitions, ARC-like operations, high-level array structure), direct-to-LLVM loses them at emission time and retrofitting a mid-level IR later is exactly the expensive path Swift took.

Transformation-shaped facts are poorly served: A001-A003 and A006 (provisional) show shape/stride, iteration-space, affine-dependence, and schedule structure living naturally in MLIR-style IRs; LLVM metadata can annotate but cannot carry restructuring intent, so tiling/fusion/layout-change optimization classes are weaker or unavailable on my path.

The corpus's own misleading-comparisons file warns that 'IR metadata solves source design' is misleading — metadata is a conditional carrier, sound only if frontend semantics and proofs are sound and preserved. My position inherits its entire soundness burden at the frontend and cannot outsource any of it to IR structure or verifiers.

The apparent evidential dominance of my position is partly selection bias: caveats.md and misleading-comparisons state the corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection, and MLIR simply lacks verified coverage. Absence of verified MLIR evidence is not evidence of MLIR's inferiority — it is a gap in the corpus.

The 'Rust ownership maps to noalias everywhere' story is explicitly flagged as misleading in the corpus: unsafe code, interior mutability, raw pointers, and callbacks complicate lowering, so 'facts true by construction' will have carve-outs where maximal metadata must be conservatively withheld.
All A-series cards I cite are direct extractions, provisional and not adversarially verified; only F-series claims carry workflow verification.
risks: Silent miscompilation from over-claiming: F003 makes violating noalias promises undefined behavior, and the pass-consumer matrix names 'false noalias causes miscompile' as the hazard. A single frontend bug in the obligation discipline produces wrong code with no diagnostic — the failure mode most at odds with this debate's soundness constraint — so the position stands or falls on a rigorous emission-obligation test regime (FileCheck metadata tests plus negative alias tests, per the matrix).

Metadata attrition inside LLVM itself: the matrix flags 'lowering strips metadata' and 'metadata dropped' as known cases even within a pure-LLVM pipeline; if LLVM's own passes routinely discard the facts we emit, the 'no intermediate layer' advantage shrinks, and we have no corpus data on survival rates.

No mid-level home for future language-specific passes: committing to one-shot emission means any later need for pre-LLVM optimization (the F009 scenario) forces either heroic frontend complexity or a costly IR retrofit.

Version drift: caveats.md flags LLVM docs (23.0.0git) and IR semantics as time-sensitive; attribute/metadata semantics changing under us is a maintenance tax borne directly by the frontend with no insulating layer.

Ceiling on loop/array performance: vectorization is conditional and cost-model dependent (A005), and without structured loop IR the language may hit a performance ceiling on affine/tensor workloads that MLIR-based competitors clear — potentially fatal for an optimizer-first language's core value proposition on those workloads.
evidenceGaps: Metadata survival data: no card quantifies which LLVM passes strip or drop alias.scope/noalias, TBAA, range, nsw, or llvm.loop metadata and how often across -O2/-O3 pipelines. The matrix marks every row 'LLVM version TBD'. This is the single most decision-relevant gap for my position and needs empirical FileCheck-based measurement on a pinned LLVM version.

Breadth gap on 'maximal metadata': F003/F004 verify only noalias and alias.scope/noalias. There are NO verified cards on TBAA, range, nsw, llvm.assume, or fast-math semantics — most of my position's named payload is uncarded and currently rests on general LLVM knowledge, not corpus evidence (A005 mentions FP flags only as vectorizer inputs, provisionally).

MLIR comparison baseline: the fit/gap document is entirely TBD and caveats.md confirms no verified MLIR coverage, so neither side of this debate can cite verified evidence about MLIR fact preservation; a head-to-head prototype (same source facts, direct-emission vs. minimal-dialect pipeline, measuring fact survival and generated-code quality) is needed before a decision.

Encodability audit: no evidence on whether the high-value array/loop facts in A001-A003 (shape, strides, iteration spaces, affine dependences) can be adequately expressed via LLVM loop metadata and assumes, or genuinely require structured IR — this determines whether my concession on transformation-shaped facts is a niche cost or a disqualifier.

Missing card series: the debate brief references N001-N006, D001-D010, C001-C006, and J001-J005, but none of the seven files I was permitted to read contain N, D, C, or J cards (only F001-F009 and A001-A010 are present). Any opposing argument built on those series is unverifiable by me and should be checked against the actual corpus files that hold them.

No quantified performance evidence anywhere: caveats.md is explicit that documentation-grounded findings do not quantify performance magnitude, compile-time cost, or code-size impact — so 'direct emission is faster to build and compiles faster' remains engineering judgment, not evidence, and I flag it as such.

result.position: Custom language-specific IR first (SIL direction): design a purpose-built, verifiable SSA IR that natively represents the language's ownership, aliasing, effect, and layout semantics; run language-level optimization and diagnostics on it; then lower to LLVM IR, emitting noalias/metadata contracts only after they have been checked. Accept maximal engineering cost for maximal fact preservation and soundness.
result.coreArgument:
The debate question is which strategy best PRESERVES source-level facts for the optimizer, and the corpus contains exactly one workflow-confirmed card whose theme is IR semantic preservation — F009 — and it points squarely at the custom-IR direction: Swift SIL is an SSA-form IR that 'keeps Swift-specific high-level semantic information', runs Swift-level optimization 'before LLVM IR generation so passes can use information LLVM would not otherwise see', and makes performance-critical semantics (ARC retain/release) explicit instructions rather than opaque runtime effects. That is the design lesson the adversarially-checked evidence actually supports: facts the optimizer needs should be first-class IR structure, not annotations.

The argument for why direct-to-LLVM-with-metadata loses facts has three evidence-grounded legs. First, the source-level facts an optimizer-first language will generate are RICHER than LLVM's vocabulary for them. F001, F005, and F006 document whole families of language-level non-interference rules — Rust &mut exclusivity, Fortran dummy-argument and TARGET rules, SPARK ownership-with-transfer and side-effect-free expressions — while F003 and F004 show that LLVM's encodings for these are essentially two mechanisms: noalias attributes/returns and alias.scope/noalias scoped metadata. The corpus's own misleading-comparisons file (notes/misleading-comparisons.jsonl) explicitly warns that 'Rust ownership automatically maps to LLVM noalias everywhere' is a misleading claim: unsafe code, interior mutability, shared references, and callbacks complicate the lowering. An ownership fact that cannot be faithfully expressed in the target IR must either be dropped (fact loss) or over-approximated into UB-bearing noalias claims (soundness hazard). A custom IR closes that gap: it represents exclusivity/ownership/effects in the language's own terms (as SIL's OSSA does per F009), optimizes against them, and only then commits to whatever conservative subset LLVM can carry.

Second, even the facts LLVM CAN express are conditional carriers, not preserved state. F003's negative scope states LLVM metadata 'is only sound if frontend obligations are true and metadata survives lowering', and the corpus's misleading-comparisons file flags 'IR metadata solves source design' as misleading for exactly this reason. The project's own pass-consumer matrix (implementation/llvm-pass-consumer-matrix.csv) lists 'lowering strips metadata' and 'metadata dropped' as failure cases for ScopedNoAliasAA and LoopVectorize, and 'false noalias causes miscompile' as the hazard. LLVM metadata is droppable by design; a fact encoded as metadata is best-effort. A fact encoded as custom-IR instruction/type structure is load-bearing: passes cannot silently discard it, and an IR verifier can re-check it after every transformation. Given the debate's ground rule that soundness may not be sacrificed, this matters doubly — the noalias contract is UB-on-violation (F003), so the strategy that emits it should be the one that can verify the obligation immediately before emission, not the one that trusts a frontend-time proof to remain true through an unverified pipeline.

Third, representation choices above LLVM level demonstrably affect what the optimizer can do, so a language that wants control must make those choices deliberately in its own IR rather than inherit them. F008 shows EH representation choice (landingpad vs funclets) changes optimization quality and register allocation; F002 shows the payoff of non-interference facts is concrete (register promotion, load/store elimination, reordering); A005 shows LoopVectorize consumes a long list of facts (dependence, alignment, FP flags, trip counts) that must all survive to the point of vectorization. The provisional A-cards further show the broader field converging on the same lesson from independent directions: MLIR memref/linalg/affine/vector (A001-A004) preserve shape, iteration-space, and vector structure as IR operations precisely because recovering them after premature lowering is hard; Halide (A006) and Futhark (A008) surface schedule and uniqueness/consumption as explicit program structure. For an optimizer-first, performance-transparent language, the custom IR also gives the natural home for diagnostics: language-level passes operating on language-level facts can explain, in source terms, why an optimization did or did not fire — LLVM remarks operate below that vocabulary (this diagnostic-quality claim is partially an evidence gap, flagged below).

Steelman summary: every alternative strategy must answer 'what happens to a fact LLVM cannot express, or expresses only as droppable metadata?' The custom-IR answer is: nothing is lost until we choose to lose it, and a verifier checks the contract at the boundary. The corpus's only confirmed IR-preservation evidence (F009) is an existence proof that this works in production at Swift scale. The cost is real and conceded — but the debate topic is fact preservation, and on that axis the custom IR is the only strategy whose fact-preservation ceiling is set by the language designers rather than by LLVM's metadata contract.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: The corpus's only workflow-confirmed card on IR semantic preservation. Directly supports the position: Swift SIL is SSA-form, keeps Swift-specific high-level semantics, runs Swift-level optimization before LLVM IR generation so passes see information LLVM would not, and makes ARC operations explicit instructions. This is a production-scale existence proof of the custom-IR-first strategy and its stated rationale.

Establishes both the target vocabulary (noalias attributes, alias.scope/noalias metadata) and its two weaknesses that motivate a custom IR: violating the contract is UB (soundness pressure), and per its negative scope the metadata is only sound if frontend obligations are true AND the metadata survives lowering — i.e., LLVM-side encoding is a conditional carrier, not preservation.

Documents the richness of source-level non-interference rules across Rust, Fortran, SPARK, and restrict-style C++ — the fact families an optimizer-first language will generate. The mismatch between this richness and LLVM's two-mechanism encoding (F003/F004) is the core fact-loss argument for an intermediate IR that can represent these rules natively.

Shows the payoff of preserved non-interference facts is concrete and broad (register promotion, redundant-read elimination, dead-write elimination, reordering), so fact loss between source and optimizer has a real optimization cost — establishing the stakes of the preservation question.

Shows LLVM's noalias return is allocation-like and very strong, but its negative scope notes source languages need lifetime/provenance/escape rules BEFORE emitting it — i.e., a language-level reasoning layer must exist somewhere; a custom IR is the natural, verifiable place for that reasoning to live and be re-checked before emission.

Fortran's dummy-argument and TARGET rules are conditional, exception-laden facts (POINTER/TARGET/coarray carve-outs) — exactly the kind of nuanced source contract that maps poorly onto a single boolean noalias annotation and benefits from native IR representation before conservative lowering.

SPARK's ownership-with-transfer and side-effect-free-expression rules are structured semantic facts (ownership state machines, effect purity) with no direct LLVM encoding at all; preserving them for optimization requires an IR whose type/instruction system can carry them, which is the custom-IR proposition.

Confirms that representation choices above the source level (landingpad vs funclet EH) materially change optimization quality and register allocation — evidence that IR representation is itself a performance decision the language should own in its own IR rather than inherit implicitly (weighted low: card's design-generalization confidence is low).

Provisional: MLIR memref keeps shape/stride/offset/memory-space and explicit no-alias contracts as optimizer-visible IR structure — independent field convergence on preserving structured memory facts above LLVM level rather than encoding them as droppable metadata.

Provisional: MLIR linalg retains iteration spaces, indexing maps, and parallel/reduction iterator kinds as operations specifically to enable tiling/fusion/vectorization — supporting the general claim that high-level structure must be preserved in IR because it is hard to recover after lowering.

Provisional: MLIR affine shows that an IR level with language-like restrictions (affine bounds/accesses) makes dependence analysis tractable — an example of optimization power coming from IR-level semantic restrictions that plain LLVM IR does not have.
