Provisional: MLIR vector's progressive lowering preserves n-D vector semantics above scalar/1-D LLVM form until late — a concrete pattern the custom IR would use: keep the semantically rich form as long as possible, lower staged.

Provisional: LLVM's vectorizers consume many fragile facts (dependence, alignment, trip counts, FP flags); every one is a fact that must survive from source to that pass, and the pass-consumer matrix lists metadata-dropping as a known failure mode — motivating a pipeline where facts are structural until the last responsible moment.

Provisional: Halide separates schedule from algorithm as explicit manipulable structure — supporting the broader lesson that optimization-relevant decisions should be reified program/IR structure, which a custom IR generalizes beyond a DSL.

Provisional: Futhark's size types and uniqueness/consumption rules show ownership-like source facts enabling copy-avoidance — another fact family with no native LLVM encoding, strengthening the claim that an optimizer-first language's fact set will exceed LLVM's vocabulary.

concessions: Cost is real and unquantified: no card measures the engineering, compile-time, or maintenance cost of a custom IR, and F009's own negative scope warns that SIL/ARC visibility is implementation-specific with OSSA/non-OSSA phase complexity and stale-docs risk. Swift needed a large, well-funded team; this position knowingly trades maximal cost for maximal preservation.

F009 is an existence proof, not a comparison: it shows SIL-style preservation works and is used, but no card quantifies how much performance Swift's SIL passes deliver versus a hypothetical rich-metadata-direct-to-LLVM Swift. Per caveats.md, documentation evidence 'usually does not quantify performance magnitude'.

The corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection (caveats.md), so my fact-loss argument leans on non-interference facts; I cannot claim from evidence that the same fact-loss pattern dominates for numerics, layout, dispatch, or concurrency.
The A-cards (MLIR, Halide, ISPC, Futhark, Chapel) are direct-extraction and provisional, not adversarially verified; I use them only as convergence signal, not as load-bearing proof.

An MLIR-hosted dialect stack could plausibly deliver much of the custom-IR benefit at lower cost, blurring the line between my position and an MLIR-based one; the corpus cannot currently distinguish them because implementation/mlir-dialect-fit-gap.md is almost entirely TBD.

A custom IR does not remove the metadata-fragility problem, it only relocates it: at the custom-IR-to-LLVM boundary we still emit noalias/metadata contracts (F003) and still depend on LLVM not dropping or misusing them; the gain is verified emission and earlier language-level use of the facts, not immunity.
risks: Pipeline duplication: a custom IR invites reimplementing passes LLVM already has (GVN/LICM/DSE analogues over language-level effects), with its own bug surface; the corpus offers no evidence on how much duplication SIL actually required or whether it paid off.

Soundness burden shifts to us: the custom IR's verifier and its lowering contracts become the trusted computing base. F003's UB-on-violation applies to whatever we emit; a bug in our ownership/effect verifier miscompiles user code, and unlike the direct-to-LLVM strategy we cannot lean on LLVM's mature verifier for our language-level invariants.

Schedule risk for an optimizer-first project: the custom IR must be designed before most language optimization work can start, front-loading the riskiest engineering; if the IR's fact vocabulary is wrong, reworking it invalidates passes built on it (Swift's OSSA migration, hinted at in F009's caveats, is a live example of such churn).

Two-level pass-ordering and phase problems: facts consumed at the custom-IR level may be transformed into forms that no longer lower to the strongest LLVM encodings, potentially LOSING backend optimization relative to a strategy that hands LLVM pristine annotated IR (no card addresses this interaction).

Diagnostics claim may overpromise: I argued language-level remarks are better on a custom IR, but no card in the read corpus compares diagnostic quality across strategies; if LLVM's remark infrastructure plus debug info suffices, one of my differentiators weakens.

The corpus's selection bias (caveats.md) could be steering the whole debate: if non-aliasing facts turn out to be less central than arrays/layout/numerics facts, the relative advantage of a custom IR could be larger or smaller than argued — direction unknown.
evidenceGaps: No head-to-head evidence comparing a custom-IR pipeline against direct-to-LLVM-with-rich-metadata on the same language: needed are benchmarks or case studies quantifying performance delta, miscompile rates, and compile-time cost (e.g., Swift with/without SIL passes, Rust MIR-opt on/off).

Metadata survival data: the pass-consumer matrix rows all say 'LLVM version TBD' and call for FileCheck/negative tests; before deciding, we need a measured inventory of WHICH LLVM passes drop alias.scope/noalias and TBAA-like metadata and under what transformations, since the direct strategy's viability hinges on it.

implementation/mlir-dialect-fit-gap.md is a stub (all TBD): whether MLIR dialects can host our fact vocabulary at lower cost than a from-scratch IR is unresolved and is the single most decision-relevant gap for choosing between my position and an MLIR-based middle path.

No corpus evidence on custom-IR verification practice: SIL's ownership verifier, its bug-catch rate, and the cost of keeping a language-level verifier sound are not covered by any read card; my soundness argument assumes verifiability that needs primary-source confirmation.

No evidence on Rust MIR, Julia SSA IR, GHC Core, or other production language-specific IRs beyond Swift SIL; a single existence proof (F009, source-accuracy high but design-generalization only medium) is thin support for a maximal-cost commitment.
No evidence on optimization-remark/diagnostic quality differences between strategies, despite performance transparency being a core language goal.

The N001-N006, D001-D010, C001-C006, and J001-J005 card series named in the ground rules were not present in any file on my assigned reading list; if they exist elsewhere in the corpus they may bear directly on this topic and should be surfaced before a decision.

Engineering-cost quantification: person-years, compile-time overhead, and maintenance burden of SIL-class IRs are entirely unmeasured in the corpus; 'maximal engineering cost' is asserted by the position statement, not bounded by evidence.

challenges.challenge: J001 reversal: the card's positive scope is one-directional — facts static languages express in types must be recovered dynamically by runtimes that lack them. It does not say every dynamically recoverable fact is statically expressible, and J002's negative scope ('deoptimization also enables optimizations static compilers cannot legally do without profiles') directly contradicts 'the fact set is the same, only the delivery differs.' Advocate 1 concedes this in concessions but the core argument's opening premise still depends on the reversed implication.

'Contracts deliver them unconditionally, in every compilation, from the first instruction executed' is contradicted by the corpus's own discipline: F003's negative scope (metadata is only sound if frontend obligations hold AND the metadata survives lowering) and misleading-comparison #5 ('IR mechanisms are conditional carriers of source facts'). F002 cards enablement of optimization classes, never guaranteed firing — whether LLVM actually promotes, eliminates, or reorders depends on uncarded cost models and pass behavior. The static channel is also conditional; only the condition differs.

The transparency axis is unevidenced even at Advocate 1's own pole: no card supports 'reading the source tells you what machine code you get' for static AOT. Static compilers' inlining, vectorization, and register-allocation heuristics are invisible in source and uncarded. The corpus supports 'contracts make facts unconditional'; it does not support 'static compilation makes performance predictable.' The whole transparency argument compares carded JIT opacity (J002/J003) against an assumed, never-carded static predictability.

Ignored caveat on J003: Advocate 1 cites it for 'cliff-shaped degradation under polymorphism and shape churn,' but J003's own negative scope says the V8 blog notes real-world performance differences from this machinery 'are often small.' The card cited for cliffs carries an explicit magnitude disclaimer that is never mentioned.

F009 backfires as cited: Swift ARC is a runtime mechanism (retain/release operations), and SIL's contribution is making those runtime effects optimizer-visible so they can be conditionally eliminated when analysis succeeds — structurally the same conditional, best-effort elimination of runtime costs that Advocate 1 condemns in HotSpot via F007/J004. The corpus's IR-architecture exemplar is a language with runtime machinery whose costs the optimizer removes fallibly, not a no-runtime-facts language.

Guards/deopt conflation: 'to use it soundly you must add guards and invalidation machinery (J002)' overstates the card. J002 documents invalidation machinery in re-specializing JITs; a plain guard branching to a statically compiled fallback needs no invalidation, no deopt metadata, no recompilation. Since Advocate 2's entire position occupies exactly that guard-without-deopt territory, much of Advocate 1's cost case (J002/J005) attacks a system no opponent is proposing.

F006 is an existence proof for a verification-oriented restricted subset, not for a general-purpose performance language — its negative scope says exactly this. The charter's ergonomics waiver covers ugliness, not expressiveness/coverage: SPARK achieves checkability by prohibiting constructs (side-effecting expressions, general aliasing), and no card evidences that a contract surface covering the full optimizer-fact inventory of a performance language is achievable. Advocate 1 flags 'unwritable vs ugly' in risks yet still deploys F006 as settling feasibility in the core argument.

The correctness/cost split leaks through inlining, on Advocate 2's own cited card: F007 documents that escape analysis results are inlining-dependent. Inlining is classified as a profile-steered 'cost' decision, but inlining decisions change which correctness facts (escape, provenance, context-sensitive aliasing) the static analyzer can derive. So what the compiler may assume becomes a function of the profile after all — the position's headline invariant ('what may be assumed is readable in source') fails without any unsoundness ever occurring, via a mechanism its own evidence documents.

Classification-by-fiat on layout: the position statement assigns 'code/data layout' to the profile-safe cost bucket, but caveats.md explicitly lists data-layout topics as having no verified coverage, and layout is not always correctness-neutral (FFI/ABI visibility, pointer identity, cross-language struct compatibility). An uncovered fact domain is baked into the position's taxonomy as if its classification were settled.

'WHICH call sites are monomorphic on a real workload is irreducibly a profile fact' (via J003) is an overclaim: in a closed-world AOT language, class-hierarchy/whole-program analysis proves many sites monomorphic statically, no profile needed. The corpus has zero devirtualization cards in either direction — Advocate 2 concedes the mechanism is uncarded, yet asserts irreducibility as fact. J003 describes a dynamic language with no static alternative available.

'A wrong or stale profile can never cause a miscompile' is a property of an architecture that does not exist yet, not of the position: Advocate 2's own risk list admits profile-to-noalias promotion is a standing temptation that 'must be enforced architecturally, not by convention.' The headline safety guarantee is a design intention presented as an established property, and the value half of the position (AOT PGO benefit) rests entirely on analogy from in-runtime JIT cards (J001/J003/J005) — their own top-listed gap.

The guard-parity claim — 'once a guard has checked the precondition, the specialized region holds the fact just as a contract-bearing region would' feeding F002's optimization classes — is the largest uncarded leap in the debate. Carded guard evidence (J001/J003) covers point-in-time checks on types, shapes, and values. Non-interference (F001/F002) is a temporal property over the region's entire execution: concurrent writers, escapes, and callbacks can violate it after the guard passes. No card shows any runtime establishing an aliasing/non-interference fact by guard, and doing so plausibly requires write-barrier-grade instrumentation the position never prices. Advocate 3 lists the lowering-soundness obligation as a gap while the core argument asserts the parity.

Convergent-evolution selection effect: CPython, V8, and PyPy (J001) converged on observe/specialize/guard because they had no static channel available. Convergence conditioned on the absence of the alternative is not evidence that a language possessing strong static contracts gains from adding speculation — it is evidence about what you do when contracts are impossible, which is a different design point from 'contracts are impractical at this module boundary.'

'Per-region costs' is asserted, uncarded, and likely false: deoptimization machinery (frame-state modeling in the IR, deopt-point constraints on register allocation and code motion, invalidation under concurrency, OSR) is global pipeline infrastructure whose presence taxes every pass, not a cost proportional to the number of designated regions. The J002/J005 costs Advocate 3 claims to confine describe whole-runtime systems, and no card supports the containment claim. Additionally, the embellishment that 'a single anomalous input can permanently degrade a region until recompilation' exceeds what J002 actually states (one-directional generalization, mechanism granularity unspecified).

False dichotomy at boundaries: the argument offers only 'assume the worst forever' or 'unsound programmer assertion' before concluding guarded speculation strictly dominates. A third carded-compatible option is never engaged: validate at ingress and construct contract-bearing typed values (parse-don't-validate) — a one-time runtime check that discharges the contract by construction, needs no deopt machinery, and is the standard AOT answer to FFI/deserialization. If ingress validation covers most 'structurally unreachable' boundary facts, the position collapses to expensive machinery for a residue, per its own final concession. The 'strict dominance' claim also fails for facts guards cannot cheaply check (temporal non-interference, absence of concurrent modification).

F008 is cited by nobody, yet it cuts across all three positions twice: (a) it is a carded optimizer-relevant fact domain (exception/cold-path representation) that is neither an aliasing contract nor a profile fact — a representation choice — showing every advocate's channel taxonomy is incomplete; (b) guard fallbacks and deopt paths are exactly the rarely-taken-path representations F008 shows can constrain hot-path optimization (funclets pinning values out of registers). The cost of representing Advocates 2's and 3's fallback paths in LLVM is a carded concern no one priced.

No advocate engages the refuted-claims discipline: caveats.md orders that the two refuted overbroad LLVM EH claims be retained 'so similar overbroad claims are not reintroduced later' and points to notes/refuted-and-unverified.jsonl. All three advocates reintroduce exactly the refuted pattern — overbroad channel-level generalizations from documentation evidence — without checking their claims against the corpus's own record of what adversarial verification kills. Also uncontested by anyone: concurrency and memory models have zero verified coverage (caveats.md), yet guard validity under concurrent mutation and contract semantics under weak memory are decisive for every position's soundness story.

Code-size symmetry: multiversioning under static contracts (Advocate 1's risk) and guard+fallback duplication (Advocates 2/3) are the same unquantified code-size cost appearing on both sides; no advocate noticed the symmetry, so it cannot differentiate positions and should be removed from all three cases until measured.

Advocate 2 alone failed to flag that the N001-N006, A001-A010, D001-D010, and C001-C006 card series named in the debate brief do not exist in the corpus files — an evidence-discipline lapse the other two advocates avoided.
result.sharpestTension: All three positions argue over which channel should carry 'optimizer-relevant facts' as if the corpus tested the channels on a common fact space — but the corpus's verified static evidence (F001/F002) covers only temporal non-interference facts, which no carded guard mechanism can check (J001/J003 guards verify point-in-time types/shapes/values, and a guard passed at region entry cannot establish that no other path writes the location during the region), while the provisional dynamic evidence covers only point-in-time facts, which contracts express trivially. The two evidence bodies are disjoint on fact class, so every load-bearing comparative claim — Advocate 1's 'the fact set is the same,' Advocate 2's clean correctness/cost separability (which leaks through profile-steered inlining per F007), and Advocate 3's 'guard-established facts feed F002's optimizations just like contracts' — lives in the uncarded gap between them. And the one number that would arbitrate the residual disagreement, the cost of a guard versus static knowledge, is explicitly flagged as unquantified in J001's negative scope and conceded as a gap by all three advocates. The debate is therefore structurally unresolvable on this corpus: the decision it feeds must either commission guard-cost and AOT-PGO/devirt evidence (the gaps all three name), or be made on charter grounds (soundness, transparency-in-kind) while explicitly recording that no performance comparison between the three positions has evidentiary support.

challenges.challenge: The 'theorems not promises' claim collapses at exactly the boundaries that matter. Shapes-in-types say nothing about whether two array VALUES overlap: A001's own negative scope states MemRef view-like operations can alias original storage and carry verifier/runtime obligations, and MemRef needed an explicit distinct_objects contract precisely because structure alone does not give cross-object disjointness. Without an ownership/uniqueness rule (A008's uniqueness is cited as precedent but never adopted into the position), the frontend cannot emit noalias for two array parameters 'as a theorem' — it is back to analysis or trust, i.e., the exact F003 situation the position claims to be the only escape from.

'Access disjointness decidable / true by construction' is uncarded and contradicted by the position's own citations. A003 claims affine restriction makes dependence analysis 'efficient and reliable' — not that disjointness holds by construction; A010 (cited by the advocate) says the restrictions are necessary-not-sufficient and compilers may still need dependence analysis, runtime checks, or serial lowering; A005 says legality may require runtime pointer checks even with structure. The core argument's 'facts as theorems' framing is internally inconsistent with the position's own concession that 'structure enables, it does not guarantee.' One of those two statements must be retracted; they cannot both anchor the case.

'The only route that satisfies the soundness constraint without trusting programmers' is false within the corpus the advocate read: F006 documents SPARK's checkable-contract route (restrictions statically verified, not trusted), and Position 2's purity route also avoids programmer trust. The uniqueness claim is rhetoric, not evidence.

Misappropriation of the verified cluster: F001 and F002 name aliasing-rule mechanisms — Rust &mut exclusivity, Fortran dummy-argument rules, SPARK anti-aliasing, restrict-like contracts. None is a shape-type or affine-index mechanism. Claiming structured arrays 'align with the corpus's strongest-attested finding' converts an analogy into an endorsement; the findings index header explicitly says workflow-confirmed does not mean design superiority, and every cited F-card carries only 'medium' design generalization.

A006 causal misuse: the card says Halide's schedule choices can be surfaced 'without changing pure algorithm semantics' — the enabler named is purity, not structured arrays. 'Only possible because the algorithm layer is structured and analyzable' is an uncarded causal claim, and since this position keeps mutation inside structured arrays, the Halide/Futhark purity-based precedents transfer less than claimed: mutation reintroduces the write-interference question that affine dependence analysis must then solve, per iteration, not 'by construction.'

Dynamic shapes and the escape-hatch boundary are unspecified where they are hardest. 'Shapes carried in types' is only argued for the static case; with runtime sizes and symbolic strides the decidability story degrades and no card covers the cost or soundness of symbolic shape reasoning (caveats.md: docs quantify nothing). Likewise: may escape-hatch code hold pointers into structured arrays? If yes, every structural noalias fact becomes conditional on unanalyzed code; if no, the hatch is too weak to absorb irregular workloads and users will abandon the structured tier — the advocate's own listed failure mode.

Internal contradiction between the soundness pitch and the performance answer: 'a wrong schedule is a performance bug, not a miscompile' holds only for a genuinely pure algorithm layer, but the position's answer to purity's copying cost is A008-style uniqueness/consumption — which reintroduces exactly the mutable-aliasing discipline the position claims to have structurally eliminated. Schedule transformations over consuming, in-place update stages are precisely where miscompiles live; the advocate lists this as a risk while the core argument continues to assert the structural-soundness slogan. You cannot claim non-interference 'by construction' and buy performance with consumption rules in the same argument.

The keystone is triply weak and the argument does not price this in: A006 is a provisional extraction from a tutorial page; caveats.md states the verified corpus does not cover Halide at all; and no card anywhere verifies that schedule application is semantics-preserving (the position's central soundness claim rests on A006's positive-scope sentence). 'Only design on the table where non-interference is structural' is an overclaim built on the corpus's thinnest evidence tier.

'MLIR Linalg is the same architecture' overreads A002: the card describes structured ops preserving iteration-space facts for compiler passes; it does not describe an algorithm/schedule separation in the IR, nor any documented lowering path from a scheduled source language through Linalg. 'The machinery already exists rather than needing invention' converts existence-of-dialects into existence-of-a-pipeline, while the dossier explicitly lists 'validate that MLIR/LLVM actually consume the preserved facts' as open. Similarly, A009 is misused: Chapel domains are first-class index sets, and the card says nothing about separating scheduling from computation.

A010 is wielded selectively: it is cited as proof that annotation-based rivals 'demonstrably underdeliver,' but its actual lesson — declared structure still requires dependence analysis, runtime checks, or conservative lowering — applies with equal force to schedule legality checking over reductions and update stages. The card cuts sideways across all structured positions, not just against the contracts rival.

Unexamined tension with the debate's own premise: a mandatory human-written schedule layer surfaces optimization decisions to programmers, not to the optimizer. With the auto-derivation half conceded to have zero cards, the position as evidenced is a human-in-the-loop tuning language — the optimizer is handed decisions, not empowered to make them. Whether that counts as 'optimizer-first' is a framing question no advocate raised and no card answers.

The opening thesis is contradicted by the advocate's own cited corpus: 'the optimizer does not consume array abstractions; it consumes legality facts' — A002 documents Linalg tiling, fusion, promotion, and vectorization consuming structured ops, indexing maps, and iterator kinds directly, and the position's own concession 1 admits pairwise noalias cannot recover that transformation space. The 'contracts are the primitive; structured arrays are sugar' hierarchy is a metaphysical claim A001 cannot bear: MemRef needing distinct_objects shows an IR without a source type system above it needs contracts; it does not show source-level structure is reducible to contracts.

F009 is inverted. The card's exemplar is Swift building SIL — a new mid-level IR created precisely because attribute-level LLVM encoding did not preserve enough source semantics to optimize. Citing F009 to argue 'no new mid-level IR to build' turns the card's lesson upside down; if anything F009 is evidence for the structured-IR positions this advocate opposes.

Uncarded IR claims stated as fact: 'align' and 'dereferenceable' attributes appear in no card in the readable corpus, and 'noalias attributes already survive to LLVM today' is asserted against F003's explicit conditionality (sound only if frontend obligations are true AND metadata survives lowering) and misleading-comparisons entry 5 ('IR mechanisms are conditional carriers of source facts'). The position's cheapest-path argument depends on precisely the unconditional survival the corpus refuses to grant.

The 'checkable' half has no evidence at the claimed generality, and the cited cards predict its collapse: F006's SPARK achieves checkability by RESTRICTING aliasing under an ownership policy, with a scope limit warning it may not map to a general-purpose performance language; F004's caveat says even noalias-return requires lifetime/provenance/escape rules before it can be emitted. Read with their caveats, the advocate's own citations imply risk 1 ('collapses into an ownership-typed design') is the likely outcome, dissolving the position's distinctness from its rivals. No card shows static discharge of disjointness over unrestricted pointers, and no data exists on what fraction of contracts would land in the unsafe-promise tier — which is F001's 'programmer truthfulness' caveat re-entering through the back door, and the number that actually decides whether this design meets the soundness bar.

All three advocates treat 'ergonomics is spendable currency' as neutralizing usability objections, but in every design soundness is mediated by human behavior: escape-hatch flight destroys P1's structural facts, hand-written schedules and boundary code carry P2's legality burden, and P3's unsafe-promise tier is a direct miscompile channel. Ergonomic collapse is therefore a soundness failure mode, not a separate cost — and caveats.md confirms ergonomics is unquantified everywhere. The pre-agreed ergonomics sacrifice cannot be invoked to wave off usability evidence gaps in an argument whose soundness depends on where humans go when the sound path hurts.

All three positions require an escape hatch or boundary with less-structured code (irregular fallback for P1, unscheduled sublanguage for P2, unsafe tier for P3), and in all three, every non-interference fact becomes conditional at that boundary. The corpus contains zero cards — verified or provisional — on boundary composition, yet each advocate's soundness argument is stated as if the optimized subset existed in isolation. Whichever position wins, the actual soundness battle will be fought at a seam nobody has evidence about.

Every advocate cites F-cards as trump evidence while ignoring the index's own demotion of them: the header states workflow-confirmed means 'source support and adversarial checkers did not refute,' not formal proof, benchmark validation, or design superiority, and every cited F-card carries only 'medium' design generalization from documentation-level sources. The debate's shared rhetorical move — 'my mechanism connects to the verified tier, therefore my array design is the sound one' — exceeds what even the verified tier can license.

Missing card series as a shared audit gap done correctly but incompletely: all three advocates note N/D/C/J cards are absent from the readable files, but none proposes suspending the parts of their argument (numerics/FP flags for vectorization legality per A005, dispatch, JIT/runtime) that those series would plausibly govern — e.g., A005's FP-math-flag dependence means the vectorization story is partly a numerics-semantics question no readable card addresses.

result.sharpestTension: All three positions anchor their soundness case in the corpus's only verified cluster — non-interference around writes (F001–F003) — yet each discharges that obligation through a different mechanism whose own cited caveats collapse it back into an undesigned ownership/uniqueness/exclusivity system: structured arrays cannot emit cross-array or view noalias facts 'as theorems' without a uniqueness rule (A001 negative scope, A008), the pure-algorithm layer buys back performance only via consumption/aliasing rules that must be checked (A008), and checkable contracts over general pointers are only known to be checkable when aliasing is restricted under an ownership policy (F006 scope limit, F004 caveat). So the debate is sequenced backwards: the verified evidence bears on the aliasing/ownership axis that nobody is debating, while the array/layout/loop axis being debated rests entirely on provisional, unverified A-cards plus two unanswered dossier questions (view/slice aliasing semantics, and whether MLIR/LLVM actually consume preserved facts). Until the ownership discipline is designed and the A-cards are adversarially verified, choosing among these three positions would decide the downstream question with the upstream one open — exactly the premature decision caveats.md exists to prevent.

challenges.challenge: The headline claim that mono's fast path is 'UNCONDITIONAL' is contradicted by the advocate's own citations. D004 documents that codegen-unit separation inhibits optimization of Rust's own monomorphized code, and D001's negative scope explicitly excludes cross-crate optimization limits. Monomorphization makes callee IDENTITY unconditional; the promised 'runtime behavior like concrete code' still depends on heuristic inlining, boundary visibility, and the same optimizer pipeline as everyone else. The unconditional/conditional dichotomy the whole case rests on is a difference of degree, not kind.

F009 is misused, not merely stretched. The card's evidence is Swift SIL — an IR that preserves generic/high-level semantics into the mid-level optimizer precisely because Swift does NOT settle dispatch in the frontend. Reading it as 'supports settling dispatch in the frontend; monomorphization is the degenerate best case' inverts the card: F009 is at least as friendly to witness-table/dictionary designs (the very counter-model the advocate admits is an open gap) as to mono-only. This inference is not flagged, unlike the F002 linkage which was.

The 'conditional recovery is fragile' attack is aimed at a strawman. D009/D010 describe Julia's JIT-style model, which no debater proposes; D007's devirtualization fragility mostly matters if you default into pervasive open dispatch, which neither rival does. Against the live rivals — the D005 hybrid (which specializes exactly where D002-style costs bite) and the unexamined Swift model — the attack reduces to the unquantified inlining-loss claim from a pedagogical source (D002, Rust Book, conceded).

D005 is mischaracterized: the card says reflection metadata 'may constrain closed-world assumptions unless restricted.' A reflection-free hybrid is a coherent variant the case never engages — the specialize/share representation choice is separable from reflection, so the hybrid is dismissed on a property it need not have.

Transparency asymmetry unaddressed: mono-only's per-call cost is legible, but its dominant costs — code size, i-cache pressure, build time (D001 negative scope; D009's over-specialization warning, itself a cross-system transfer from Julia) — are global, emergent, and invisible at any call site. Judged by its own prime directive ('cost model readable from source'), the position fails on exactly the cost axis where it is weakest, while penalizing rivals for the equivalent failure.

The single restricted dyn construct must carry heterogeneity, closures-adjacent patterns, and the dossier's dispatch-heavy interop targets (JVM/.NET, Python extension APIs). D003 evidences only that Rust's exact dyn-compatibility line is coherent; there is no card that a stricter construct is viable, and the advocate concedes the 'stricter' half is pure conjecture — meaning the position's load-bearing escape hatch is its least-evidenced component.

The soundness framing is a category error and misreads D007. D007 documents GUARDED speculative devirtualization — sound by construction via runtime checks, neither 'conservative' nor 'trust-based.' So the trichotomy 'open-world devirtualization is either conservative or trust-based' is false on the advocate's own card, and soundness cannot be the decisive point: the only unsound mechanism in evidence (-fno-semantic-interposition's assumption, D008) is proposed by nobody in this debate. The real differentiator is predictability — where this position has its own unacknowledged hole.

Edit-at-a-distance transparency failure, unstated anywhere in the case: under whole-program devirtualization plus sealed-by-default, whether a call site compiles to a direct call depends on GLOBAL facts (how many implementations exist anywhere in the program). Adding one type in one module silently flips call sites program-wide from direct to indirect. The claim 'every call site is either provably direct or explicitly written as dynamic' is unsupported — closed world makes the target SET known, not singular — and local source-readability of cost, the position's stated prime directive, is structurally violated.

'Compilers demonstrably eliminate it — but only under cross-module visibility' overstates D007: the card documents devirtualization mechanisms generally, with LTO/IPA INCREASING cross-file visibility, not gating the mechanism. Meanwhile 'mandatory LTO simply bakes in the favorable side' converts D004's documented tradeoff KNOB into a fixed cost of unquantified magnitude (caveats.md: no compile-time data anywhere), with no treatment of dev/release build-mode divergence — if dev builds skip whole-program analysis, the language has two performance classes by build mode, another transparency breach.

F007's design-generalization is rated 'low' in the confirmed-findings index, and it concerns HotSpot escape analysis, not dispatch. Using it as load-bearing 'pattern evidence' for dispatch policy ignores the index's own rating and caveats.md's warning that the corpus's managed-runtime coverage is selection-biased — the same discipline the advocate invokes against rivals.

The ban list (interposition, reflection, dynamic loading) is the position's load-bearing precondition and has zero cards on enforceability: D008's negative scope explicitly says the source language must separately choose its module/ABI rules, and every interop target in the dossier (Python extension APIs, JVM/.NET, GPU runtimes, system dynamic libraries) imports openness at the boundary. The advocate concedes the boundary layer is undesigned — but then the headline soundness-by-construction benefit is currently a conjecture about an unspecified design, and the conceded risk ('any leak converts optimization into miscompilation') makes this the only position whose failure mode is the one outcome the ground rules forbid.

The anchor does not transfer. D005 documents a JIT runtime with lazy instantiation and runtime dictionaries; every AOT-relevant property the case needs — 'a stable, separately-compilable artifact,' shared bodies crossing ABI boundaries, LLVM-AOT compatibility — is asserted with no card, and the conceded NativeAOT gap sits directly under the word 'decisive.' The rhetorical advantage claimed ('the only position directly attested as a shipped design') borrows credibility from enabling mechanisms (runtime codegen, lazy instantiation) the proposed core will not have.

D008 is double-edged and used one-sided: semantic interposition restricts IPA, inlining, and propagation for exported symbols — which means an EXPORTED shared generic body loses precisely the D007 devirtualization/indirect-inlining recovery the shared path's performance story depends on. The card undermines the hybrid's shared path across dynamic-linking boundaries at least as much as it undermines monomorphization, yet it is cited only against the rival.

Cost-class conflation on the position's strongest leg: D002 documents vtable lookup cost and blocked inlining, not boxing. The claim that sharing value-type instantiations would inherit 'the same class of cost' smuggles allocation, GC-pressure, and pointer-chasing costs into a card that does not cover them — an unflagged inference, and the nearest allocation-cost card (F007, conditional escape-analysis recovery) is not engaged.

The hybrid inherits exactly the conditionality it markets itself as avoiding: the shared path's acceptability rests on D007 recovery that the advocate's own citations describe as option-dependent, revertible, and boundary-fragile (D004, D008). 'Hard, source-visible rule' describes the representation split only — the COST of the shared half remains conditional on the same recovery machinery Advocate 1 attacks. The transparency claim covers half the model.

'Decisive for a language that must interoperate across ABI boundaries (the dossier's central concern)' treats research scaffolding as a requirements document. caveats.md states implementation files 'should constrain later debates by asking what can be represented... not decide'; the dossier lists topics and targets, it establishes no requirement that the performance core ship generics across stable ABI boundaries. The position's decisive advantage is grounded in a requirement nobody has decided exists.

The proposed explicit value-generic/ref-generic surface forces every library author to choose a representation per API against an upstream value/reference-type decision that is explicitly unresolved — the position pre-decides the type-system dichotomy through the back door (partially conceded) and creates a combinatorial API-design and evolution burden (changing a type's kind changes the performance class of every generic API it flows through) that no card addresses and the case never prices.

unsupportedClaims: A1: 'monomorphization's fast path is UNCONDITIONAL — no heuristic, no profile, no whole-program pass required for the common case to be fast' — contradicted by D001's negative scope (cross-crate limits uncovered) and D004 (codegen-unit boundaries inhibit optimization of monomorphized code); inlining remains heuristic in all cases.

A1: 'F009 supports settling dispatch in the frontend; monomorphization is the degenerate best case' — F009 evidences semantic PRESERVATION into a mid-level IR (Swift SIL), whose exemplar language does not monomorphize by default; the card does not support early resolution.

A1: 'an opaque indirect call is a wall behind which the compiler must conservatively assume interference' — self-flagged as inference; no card (F002 does not discuss call boundaries). Correctly flagged, but it remains the load-bearing link between the confirmed F-layer and the dispatch position.

A1: 'going stricter than Rust merely completes the transparency contract Rust started' — no card evidences tightening beyond D003's exact line; conceded as conjecture yet retained in the position statement.

A2: 'the corpus shows the only sound way to [make dispatch compile-time provable] is to close the world' — no exclusivity evidence anywhere; guarded speculative devirtualization (D007) and deopt-based JIT models (explicitly open in the dossier) are sound open-world alternatives.

A2: 'open-world devirtualization is either conservative or trust-based' — refuted by A2's own D007 citation, which documents guarded speculative devirtualization: neither conservative nor trust-based.

A2: 'under my model every call site is either provably direct or explicitly written as dynamic' — closed world with multiple live implementations still yields indirect calls; no card supports universal provable directness.
A2: 'compilers demonstrably eliminate it... but only under cross-module visibility' — 'only' overstates D007, which documents devirtualization with LTO/IPA increasing (not gating) visibility.

A3: 'a shared reference-type body is a stable, separately-compilable artifact — decisive for a language that must interoperate across ABI boundaries' — no AOT or ABI card exists (D005 is a JIT runtime); the 'must interoperate' requirement is read out of a scaffolding dossier that caveats.md says must not decide design.

A3: 'D007 documents that compilers can claw much of that back' — 'much' is a magnitude claim; caveats.md states documentation evidence does not quantify performance magnitude, and D007's negative scope notes speculation may be reverted.

A3: sharing value-type instantiations inherits 'the same class of cost D002 attributes to dynamic dispatch' — D002 covers vtable lookup and blocked inlining, not boxing/allocation/GC costs; unflagged cost-class transfer.

A1 and A3 (shared): transferring D009's over-specialization warning (Julia's runtime specialization heuristics) to AOT monomorphization code-size/i-cache claims is a cross-system inference with no supporting card — used by A1 as a risk against itself (honestly) and by A3 as affirmative motivation (not licensed).

All three: every advocate's core dispatch case rests exclusively on D-cards that are direct-extraction, provisional, and drawn from pedagogical/option documentation (Rust Book, MS Learn, GCC manual, Julia manual); not one dispatch claim in this debate is workflow-confirmed. Per caveats.md's standing rule, no position can currently claim to be 'best evidenced' — only differently defaulted.
missingConsiderations: Decision-space decomposition: the three packages overlap heavily (all monomorphize value-type/most generics, all restrict dynamic dispatch, all want finality facts). The genuine independent axes — (a) reference-instantiation representation (specialize vs share), (b) whole-program visibility (mandatory vs optional knob), (c) core bans on reflection/loading/interposition, (d) explicit-dyn surface syntax — are orthogonal and mixable (e.g., mono-only + sealed-by-default + optional LTO is a coherent fourth position). No advocate mapped this, so the debate compares package deals instead of decisions.

Closures and function pointers: every model retains indirect calls through first-class functions; the dossier lists closures as a topic, no card covers them, and every 'cost readable from source' claim silently excludes the most common source of indirection in real code.

GPU/accelerator interop (a dossier target): device code generally cannot perform indirect dispatch, structurally forcing monomorphization for kernels regardless of the host-side model — a constraint that cuts across all three positions and was never raised by anyone.

'Performance transparency' was never operationalized, yet all three advocates invoke it as the prime directive: per-call local cost favors mono-only; global emergent costs (code size, i-cache, whole-program analysis outcomes) disfavor each position differently; stability of cost under distant edits disqualifies naive closed-world devirtualization. Each advocate implicitly chose the definition their position wins under.

Dev/release build-mode divergence: D004 documents the visibility/compile-time tradeoff as a knob; nobody addressed whether the cost model must hold in incremental development builds, which both mandatory-LTO and heavy monomorphization degrade — potentially creating two performance classes by build mode.

Alias-fact precision as a first-class decision criterion: specialized bodies can carry instantiation-specific noalias facts, while a shared body must emit only the intersection of facts valid for all instantiations (F002/F003 territory — the corpus's ONLY confirmed high-value layer, and F003 warns unsound metadata is IR-level UB). This is the one place the confirmed evidence directly bears on the generics choice; it appears only as a defensive footnote in Advocate 3's case and was never developed by the position it favors (Advocate 1). A targeted evidence card here is arguably the cheapest high-value gap to close.

Process defect, confirmed by critic verification: the ground rules cite card series N001-N006, A001-A010, C001-C006, and J001-J005, but the five listed files contain only F001-F009 and D001-D010. All advocates flagged this; direct inspection confirms it. The decision record must reconcile the ground-rules card list with the actual corpus before any position is scored, and caveats.md's synthesis-critique rules (do not treat absent evidence as settled) apply directly.

No advocate proposed a decision protocol matching the evidence state: given zero quantitative cards and three explicitly-open counter-models (Swift witness tables/resilience, .NET NativeAOT, HotSpot CHA+deopt — all named 'Open' in the dossier), the honest output of this debate is a provisional default plus mandatory evidence tasks and tripwires (e.g., 'mono-only default, revisit if Swift-resilience evidence shows viable stable-ABI generics'), not a winner.
result.sharpestTension: The debate's real fault line is an upstream requirement question no evidence card addresses: must the performance core ship and consume separately-compiled, stable-ABI generic and dispatched code (binary libraries, plugins, dynamic linking)? If yes, monomorphization-only and mandatory whole-program compilation are structurally disqualified (both advocates concede this is their potentially fatal risk) and a shared-body representation becomes near-mandatory; if no, the hybrid's chief motivation and the closed-world model's boundary layer largely evaporate and mono-only wins by default. The corpus documents only the negative half — that boundaries break optimization (D004, D008) — and contains zero evidence on any mechanism that carries generic code across a boundary well: Swift witness tables/resilience, .NET NativeAOT, and HotSpot CHA-with-deoptimization are all explicitly 'Open' in the dossier. Compounding this, all three advocates justify their packages by 'performance transparency' while each conceals a non-local cost that violates it — mono-only's emergent code-size/i-cache footprint, closed-world's edit-at-a-distance direct-to-indirect flips, and the hybrid's option-dependent recovery on its shared path. The dispatch model therefore cannot be chosen on this corpus; only a default with tripwires can be, and the single highest-leverage action is closing the Swift/NativeAOT/HotSpot gap plus obtaining any quantitative monomorphization-bloat and devirtualization-effectiveness data before the decision phase.
